MEMORANDUM  IN  OPPOSITION  TO
PETITIONS  FOR  WRITS  OF  CERTIORARI

As set forth  hereafter,  respondents  Bradley,  et al.  (plain-
tiffs in this  cause),  submit  that  this  school  segregation  case

2

is  not  in  an  appropriate  posture  for  Supreme  Court  mr
view."

Basically,  the  petitioners  seek  review  of  interlocutory
remedial  orders  in advance  of the  framing  and  adoption of
a remedial  plan and  evidentiary  hearings  in the  trial court.
No  actual  plan of desegregation  has  been  approved  or even
considered  by  the  courts  below;  no  defendant  has  bee
ordered  to  do  anything  except  to  participate  fully  in plan.
ning  and  to  join  in  the  hearing  in  the  district  court,  The
Court  of Appeals-has  affirmed  the  findings  of constitutional
violation  and  of inadequacy  of relief  limited  to  the  Detroit
school  system,  directed  that  plaintiffs  amend  their  con-
plaint  to  conform  to  the  evidence,  required  joinder  of any
school  district  potentially  affected  by any  plan,  and ordered
that  a full  hearing  on  remedy  be held  and  that  the  Legisla-
ture  of  the  State  of  Michigan  be  given  an  opportunity  to
act  before  any  plan  of  desegregation  is  devised  and  ap
proved  by the  district  court.

Proceedings  are  underway  in  the  district  court  in com-
pliance  with  these  directions.  The  court  has  ordered  that
school  districts  (and  the  chief  school  officials  of  districts)
which  may  possibly  be  affected  by  any  plans  eventually
adopted,  be  joined  as  parties  defendant  so  that  they may
be  heard.  Plaintiffs  have  filed  an  amended  complaint  to
conform  to the  evidence  and  seeking  full  relief;  the  opinion
of the  Court  of Appeals  has  been  formally  transmitted  by
the  petitioner  Attorney  General  to  the  Michigan  Legisls  -
ture  for  its  consideration.

1 We  have  chosen  not  to submit  a lengthy  Brief  in Opposition to

Certiorari  correcting  petitioners’  various  omissions  of fact  and mis-
characterizations.  In  our  view,  the  real  issues,  if any  remain,  wil
appear  only after the actions  and hearings  directed  by the Court
of Appeals  are  concluded,  and  a decision  has  been  rendered  by the
district  court and reviewed  by the Court  of  Appeals.

3

In  such  circumstances,  this  court  cannot  properly  con-
sider  the very  issues  which  are  essential  to  review  in a case
like this,  As yet, there  are  no  plans,  nor  hearing  on  remedy,
nor  findings  and  conclusions  thereon  by the  district  court,
nor  review  of any  remedial  orders  by the  Court  of Appeals.
This  Court  is in no  position  to evaluate  the  practicalities  of
the  local  situation,  the  constitutional  effectiveness  of  al-
ternative  plans  of  remedy,  the  extent  and  form  of  the
remedy,  the  extent  of  transportation  and  administrative
reorganization  required,  and  the  precise  manner  in  which
school district  boundaries  need be permeated  for the  limited
purpose  of accomplishing  complete  relief.

Petitioners,  and  all  other  school  districts  which  may  be
affected  by any  plan,  as  well  as  all  other  parties  to  this
litigation,  and  the  Michigan  state  officials,  now  have  the
opportunity  in the  courts  below  to present  evidence  and  ar-
gument  which  will  shape  a  complete  record  in  this  cause.’

*In  remanding  this  case  the  Court  of  Ap

directed  the  dis-
whom  relief  is sought,
trict  court  to  afford  to  “any  party  against
including  school  districts  which  heretofore  have  intervened  and
school  districts  which  hereafter  may  become  parties . . .  an  oppor-
tunity to  offer  additional  evidence,  and  to  cross-examine  available

pleadings,  including  amendments  thereto,  as may  be  relevant  and
admissible  to such  issues.”  This  direction is  in  accord  with  the  tra-
ditional  legal principles  under  which  federal  courts  have  always

that  the district  court  should  and  will  Mow  the  added  defendants
to develop  all  relevant  evidence  necessary  for  decision  and  deter-
mination Mf the issues  both  of violation  and  of remedy.  Petitioners
correctly  note  that  the  Court  of  Appeals  held  that  the  district

present  evidence  on  this  issue ; but in light of this Court’s decision
(1973),  and
in Keyes v. School  Dist. No.  1, Denver,

US.

4

No  one  can  foretell  what  contested  issues  will  thereafter
remain  among  the  parties,  nor  what  record  evidence  wil]
define  those  issues.’  It may  well  be  that  after  the  hearings
in the  district  court,  the  form,  scope  and  indeed  the  souree
of  the  remedy  would  be  markedly  different  from  that  de
scribed  in  the  petitions.‘  Under  these  circumstances,  dec.
sion  of  the  issues  presented  in  the  petitions  at  this  stage
of  the  proceedings  would  virtually  require  an  advisory
opinion.  Judicial  economy  and  the  established  practice  of
this  Court  require  that  the  district  court  decide,  and  the
Court  of Appeals  review,  these  issues  based  on  the relevant
evidence  presented  by  all  of  the  parties,  prior  to  this
Court’s  review.

There  is no  reason  to depart from  this  Court’s  established
practice  of  refusing  to  review  school  segregation  cases  in

of the  evidence  already  introduced  documenting  the  use  by Detroit
and  Michigan  school  officials  of  virtually  all  of  the  classic  segre.
gating  techniques  which  have  been  identified  by  this  and  other
Courts,  and  since  petitioners  lack  the  power  to  rewrite  history,  the
district  court’s  findings  of  violation  seem  likely  to  be  reaffirmed.
To  date,  the  original  intervening  school  districts  have  never  sug-
gested  what  evidence  they  can  or  would  present  on  this  issue.

* The  district  court  will  of  course  consider  the  evidence  already
introduced  in  this  record  at  the  lengthy  trial;  similarly,  it wil
evaluate  any  other  evidence  to  be  presented  by the: parties  which
might  lead  it to  modify,  amend  or  supplement  its  original  rulings.
New  evidence  concerning  the  practicality  and  efficacy  of proposed
specific  remedial  techniques  will  of  necessity  be  presented  by all
parties,  including  (if  they  so  desire),  evidence  by  petitioners  in-
tended  to  demonstrate  the  impracticality  of any  remedy  extending
beyond  the  present  boundaries  of  the  Detroit  school  district.

‘For  example,  in  a  report  to  the  district  court,  petitioner  State
Superintendent  of  Public  Instruction  recommended  the  exchange
of pupils  by contract  among  existing  school  districts,  at least as a
interim  measure.  Plaintiffs  supported  this  recommendation  below
as  a  workable  method  of  proceeding  to  accomplish  complete  relief
from  the  constitutional  violations  with  as  little  intrusion  as  po
sible  into  the  State’s  existing  internal  structures  for  administering
public  education.  Compare  Bradley  v.  State  Bd.  of Educ.,  462 F2
1058,  1066-67  (4th  Cir.  1972),  afd  by  an  equally  divided  cout,

U.S.

(1973).

5

the absence  of a plan,  especially  when  remedial  proceedings
are  underway  below,  the  final  outcome  is uncertain,  and  the
resolution  of issues  depends  upon  the evidentiary  contribu-
tions  both  of petitioners  and  of  newly  added  parties.  No
substantial  harm  will  be  visited  upon  petitioners  should
this  Court  decline  review.  If  after  the  completion  of  the
proceedings  below,  any  party  is  still  dissatisfied  with  the
final determination  and  orders,  recourse  may  be  had  to  the
Court  of Appeals,  and  if necessary,  to this  Court.  With  the
benefit  of the  rulings  of the  lower  courts,  this  Court  could
better determine  whether  any  remaining  disputed  issue  pre-
sented  by the completed  record  is worthy  of review.

CONCLUSION

Wuenerore,  for  the  foregoing  reasons,  these  respondents
Bradley,  et al. respectfully  pray  that  the  petitions  for writs
of certiorari  be denied.

IN THE  SUPREME  COURT  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES

WILLIAM  G. MILLIKEN,  et al,

PETITIONERS’  REPLY

ARGUMENT

Respondents,  Bradley,  et  al,  attempt  to  persuade  this
Court  to  refrain  from  reviewing  this  case  on  the  basis  that
the orders  below  are  interlocutory.  Nothing could  be further
from  the  truth.  In its order  dated  July  20, 1972,  the  District
Court  determined  as  final,  under  Rule  54(b)  of FR  Civ  P,
its  Ruling  on  Issue  of  Segregation,  its  Findings  of  Fact
and  Conclusions  of Law  on  Detroit-Only  Plans  of Desegre-
gation,  its Ruling  on  Propriety  of Considering  a  Metropoli-
tan  Remedy,  and  the  Ruling  for  Desegregation  Area  and
Development  of Plans,  among  others,  and  certified  as  con-
trolling  questions  of  law  the  issues  presented  therein  to
the  Court  of  Appeals,  under  the  provisions  of  28  USC
1292(b).  (118a)]  The  Court  of  Appeals  affirmed  the
Ruling  on  Issue  of  Segregation  and  the  Findings  of  Fact

t)

_

Hereafter  references  to  the  Joint  Appendix  filed  heretofore  will

mabe  indicated  by  page  numbers  enclosed  in  parentheses.

a  ate

and  Conclusions  of Law  on  Detroit-Only  Plans  of Desegre.
gation,  and  affirmed  in  principle  the  Ruling  on  Propriety
of a Metropolitan  Remedy  and  the  Ruling  on  Desegregation
Area  and  Development  of Plans,  but  vacated  the  same  for
non-joinder  of affected  school  districts  as  necessary  parties,
(189a-190a)  On these  questions,  controlling  and fundamental
to the  further  conduct  of this  case,  the  decision  of the Court
of Appeals  is final  and  binding  upon  petitioners  unless  re.
viewed  now  by this  Court  and  reversed.  Gillespie  v  United
States  Steel  Co, 379 US  148,  154  (1964).  See  also  dissenting
opinion  of  Judge  Weick.  (206a-207a).

Respondents,  Bradley,  et  al,  suggest  that  the  newly
added  school  districts  and  the  petitioner  intervening  school
districts  may,  upon  proper  showing,!*]  present  evidence
going  to  the  violation  and  sufficiency  of  the  Detroit-Only
remedy  if  the  District  Court  chooses  to  admit  such  evi-
dence.  Yet,  in  their  amended  complaint  to  conform  to the
evidence,  paragraphs  10, et seq,  they assert  that  the  Detroit

[2]

Respondents  do  not  reveal  their  definition  of  the  phrase  “upon
proper  showing.”  Due  process,  at  a  minimum,  requires  that  a  de
fendant  school  district  be  given  notice  of  the  charges  against  it,
the  right  to  be  present  by  counsel,  the  right  to  offer  evidence,  the
right  to  cross-examine  opposing  witnesses  and  the  right  to  be heard
on  all  issues—factual  and  legal—that  affects  it.  The  exercise  of
these  rights  is  not  dependent  “upon  a  proper  showing,”  regardless
of  how  this  term  is  defined.  It  is  precisely  these  rights  that  are
denied  the  added  and  petitioner  intervening  school  districts  by the
Court  of  Appeals’  affirmance  of  the  District  Court’s  Rulings  on  the
Issue  of  Segregation  and  “Detroit-Only”  plans  of  desegregation
(178a)  and  the  affirmance  in  principle  of  the  District  Court’s  Rul
ing  on  Propriety  of  Metropolitan  Remedy  (177a).

The  gratuitous,  undefined  phrase  “upon  a  proper  showing”  is
dissembling  in  two  respects:  (1)  it  conceals  the  lack  of  due  process
in  the  District  Court,  and  (2)  it  suggests  the  existence  of factual
issues  which,  in  fact,  are  foreclosed  by  the  Court  of  Appel
affirmance  of  the  District  Court’s  rulings.

ae tm

school  district  is segregated  and  a  multi-district  remedy  is
required  ‘‘wholly  apart  from  the  actions  of  the  suburban
defendants  or  any  of  them.’’  Thus,  unless  this  Court
grants  certiorari  and  reverses  and  remands  the  decision
of the  Court  of  Appeals,  the  suggestion  of  respondents,
Bradley,  et al, is “wholly  illusory  with  respect  to the  issues
of  segregation,  the  ‘Detroit-Only  plan’  and  the  ‘Metro-
politan  plan’,  as  the  opinion  expressly  excludes  these
issues  from  reconsideration  upon  the  remand.’’  Opinion  ..
of Judge  Weick.  (206a)

Even  though  all  of  the  school  districts  in  the  tri-county
area  of Wayne,  Macomb  and  Oakland,  with  the  exception  of
Pontiac  school  district,  are  now  parties  in  this  action,  the
decision  of the  Court  of  Appeals  and  Amended  Complaint
to Conform  to Evidence  of respondents,  Bradley,  et al, fore-
close  litigation  of any  factual  issue  as  a  predicate  to multi-
district  relief.  The  legal  predicates,  segregation,  insuffi-
ciency  of  a  Detroit-Only  plan  and  propriety  of  a  multi-
district  remedy  have  been  finaily  and  adversely  determined.
The multi-district  scope  of the remedy  has  been  determined.
The multi-district  form  of the  remedy  has  been  determined.

Before  hearings  in  the  District  Court  commence  and
these  districts  are  put  to  the  expense  of  marshaling  their
efforts  to  hearings  necessarily  limited  by  the  decision  of
the  Court  of  Appeals  to  the  extent  of  the  multi-district
remedy,  and  such  other  issues  as  the  District  Court  by
grace  may  permit,  as  contrasted  with  fulfilling  their  pri-
mary  mission  of  educating  resident  children  within  each
district,  this  Court  should  grant  certiorari,  receive  briefs
and  hear  oral  arguments  to  dispose  of  the  paramount  is-
sues  of  constitutional  law  raised  by  the  petitions  for
certiorari.

_  Respondents,  Bradley,  et  al, at  p 3, footnote  2, acknowl-

a eae

edge  and  unsuccessfully  attempt  to, paper  over  the  lack
of  fundamental  due  process  afforded  the  intervening  and
added  ‘school  district  defendants  herein.  Due  process  jg
not  the  opportunity,  dependent  upon  the  grace  of the  trial
judge,  to  relitigate  issues  already  finally  and  adversely
determined  by the  trial  court  and  the  Court  of Appeals,  as
proposed  by respondents.  Rather,  due  process  is the  right
to notice  and  opportunity  to be  heard,  as  a  matter  of right,
at  a  meaningful  stage  of  the  proceedings.  (212a)  (235a.
236a)  (239a-240a)

The  proper  procedure,  where  a  multi-school  district
remedy  is sought,  is joinder  of all potentially  affected  school
districts,  with  full  rights  of  participation,  prior  to  the
initial  trial  on  the  merits.  See  Higgins  v  Board  of Educa
tion  of  the  City  of  Grand  Rapids,  Michigan,  (WD,  OA
6386),  Slip  Opinion  of  Judge  Albert  J.  Engel,  July  18,
1973,  where  this  procedure  was  employed.

Respondents,  Bradley,  et al, are  seeking  a  massive  multi-
school  district  remedy  involving  the  expensive  transporta-
tion  of  school  children  across  school  district  and  county
lines,  together  with  faculty  reassignment  and  major  altera-
tions  in the  present  legal  and  administrative  arrangements
governing  Michigan  school  districts,  all  for  the  purpose  of
some  artificial  racial  balance.  The  children,  parents  and
school  officials  in  each  affected  school  district  will  bear
the  full  burden  of  implementing  respondents’  social  goal.
Thus,  at a minimum,  these  hundreds  of thousands  of people
deserve  a  fair  opportunity  to  be  heard  on  all  issues.  A
major  reason  that  the  American  people  comply  with  un-
popular  federal  court  orders  is that  they  view  the  federal
judicial  process  as  a  fundamentally  fair  process.  Here,  a8
to  the  affected  school  districts,  simple  fairness  requires  full
opportunity  to  be  heard  on  all  issues,  as  a  matter  of right,

een Se

not  of  grace.  (240a)  Otherwise  the  proceedings  remain
fatally  defective.

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  a  writ  of  certiorari  should
issue  to review  the  decision  of  the  Sixth  Circuit  rendered
herein  on  June  12, 1973.

Respectfully  submitted,

FRANK  J.  KELLEY

school  district  geographical  area  is, per  se,  unconstitutional;  ine
spective  of intention  to  segregate  or  causal  connection  therewith.

an  unprecedented  nature.  It  is  therefore  respectfully  submitted
that  review  of  the  question  presented  at  this  time  is not  only

execution  of certain  provisions  of the  Michigan  Constitution  and  statutes  of
statewide  application,  in  pursuance  of which  all independent  Michigan  school
.  districts  are  organized  and  operated.  The  Court  of Appeals,  however,  chose  to
ally  ignore  this  problem  notwithstanding  the  provisions  of  28  U.S.C.

2

The  undersigned  as  counsel  for  the  above-named  or-
ganization,  respectfully  moves  this  Court  for  leave  to  file
the  accompanying  brief  amicus  curiae.

Interest  of  the  Amicus.

The  Jewish  Rights  Council,  Amicus,  was  founded  in
1971  for  the  purpose  of  ensuring  and  promoting  equality
of  all  persons  before  the  law,  and  in  that  manner  to
strengthen  and  preserve  the  security  and  constitutional
rights  of Jews  in America  through  the  preservation  of the
equal  rights  of all  Americans.  The  Jewish  Rights  Council
believes  that  the  welfare  of  Jews  in  this  country  is  in-
separably  related  to  and  dependent  upon  the  equality  of
treatment  of  all  Americans,  regardless  of  race,  religion,
creed,  or  ethnic  background.
The  membership  of  the
Jewish  Rights  Council  includes  over  one  hundred  Rabbis
from  over  thirteen  states,  representing  Orthodox,  Con-
servative  and  Reform  Congregations.
The  leadership  of
the  Jewish  Rights  Council  includes  Rabbis  who  marched
with  the  late  Reverend  Martin  Luther  King  in  Selma,
Alabama,  for  the  equality  of  all  persons  before  the  law.

The  instant  case  raises  an  important,  if  not  crucial,
issue  under  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  of  the  Four-
teenth  Amendment,  involving  the  imposition  of  a  Metro-
politan  Plan  of  cross-district  pupil  assignments  in  the
public  schools  of  a  major  metropolis  in  order  that  no
public  “school,  grade  or  classroom  b[e]  substantially  dis-
proportionate  to  the  overall  pupil  racial  composition.”
District  Court  below,  Joint  Appendix  (101a-102a).*

Public  education  has  been  one  of  the  keystones  for  the
success  of the  Jew  and  other  minority  groups  in  America,
and  therefore  the  Jewish  Rights  Council  has  a  particular

*Hereinafter,  references  to  the  Joint  Appendix,  filed  by
petitioners  with  their  petitions  for  writs  of  certiorari,  will  be
indicated  by  page  numbers  (followed  by  the  letter  ‘“‘a’’)
en-
closed  in  parentheses  (  ).  ©

3

interest  in  quality,  as  well  as  equality,  in  public  educa-
tional  facilities.  Being  composed  of  members  of  an  his-
torically  persecuted  minority,  the  membership  of the  Jew-
ish  Rights  Council  is  especially  sensitive  to  any  and  all
forms  of  discrimination  based  upon  race,  religion,  or
Therefore,  the  interest  of  Amicus
ethnic  background.
herein  is  to  oppose  the  misinterpretation  of  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of the  Constitution  as  requiring  a  racial
group  balancing  in  the  public  schools  or  in  any  other
public  function  in  this  country.  The  judicial  mandate  of
a color-conscious  racial  balancing  in public  school  systems
threatens  to  undermine  the  principles  underlying  a  color-
blind  merit  system  of  entry  into  such  fields  as  higher
education  and  employment,  by way  of “affirmative  action”
programs  in favor  of certain  minority  groups  in the  name
of racial  balancing,  to  the  detriment  of  the  equal  protec-
tion  and  opportunity  of members  of those  minority  groups
These
which  are  not  accorded  the  same  preferences.
“efirmative  action”  preferences  pose  “intractable  prob-
lems”  of  values  underlying  our  society  and  conflict  with
the  fundamental  “belief  in  individual  human  dignity  and
the  libertarian  belief  that  each  person  is  entitled  to  be
judged and  valued  as  an  individual.”  (Appendix  A  in dis-
senting  opinion  of  Cirenit  Judge  Weick  in  this  case  in
the  Court  below,  Joint  Appendix  (191la,  271a).  And  yet
the  District  Court  below  found  the  need  for  just  such
an  “affirmative  program  for  black  employment”  (76a)
with  respect  to  the  hiring  and  promotion  of  black  staff
and  teachers,  as  part  and  parcel  of  the  Metropolitan
Plan.

However,  the  Jewish  Rights  Council  firmly  believes
that  it is extremely  important  for  the  preservation  of  our
American  way  of  life,  with  its  concomitant  “American
dream,”  that  the  rights  of every  person  shall  be  accorded
on  an  individual  basis,  and  that  the  rights  of “any  person”
(Amend.  XTV)  shall  not  be  sacrificed  in  the  name  of
the  promotion  of  a  selected  group.  But  the  approach  of
the  District  Court  that  “no  school,  grade.  or  classroom

—

4

bfe}  substantially  disproportionate
to the overall  pupil
racial  composition”  (101a-102a)  is but  a  small  step  from
the infamous  numerus  clausus  of the dark  past  under
which  Jews  and  other  minorities  suffered.

On  the  other  hand,  the  Jewish  Rights  Council  wishes
to make  it  crystal  clear  that  there  must  never  be the
slightest  retreat  from  the  principle  of  Brown  v.  Board
of Education,  347  U.  8S. 483  (1954),  that  a  State  or  its
agents  may  not  deny  the  equal  access  to  a  public  school
(or  other  facility)  to  any  person  on  the  basis  of  race,
religion,  or  ethnic  background.  In  the  words  of  the  late
Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter :

“Color  alone  cannot  bar  a  child  from  a  public
school.”  Cooper  v.  Aaron,  358  U.  S.  1,  25  (1958)
(concur.  op.).

This  is  the  promise  of  Brown  from  which  there  must  be
no  retreat  anywhere  in  our  Nation.

The  Jewish  Rights  Council  has  filed  briefs  as  amicus
curiae  in  this  Court  previously  in  the  public  school  cases
of  Keyes  v.  School  District  No.  1,  Denver,  Colorado,
Docket  No.  71-507,  and  in  Richmond  School  Board  v.
Board  of Education,  412  U.  S.  92;  and  is  filing  a  brief
amicus  curiae  this  Term  in  DeFumis  v.  Odegaard,  No.
73-235,  on  consent  of  all  parties.

The  consent  of the  parties  to  the  filing  of  a  brief  amicus
curiae  has  been  sought  by  the  undersigned.
Counsel  for
the  petitioners  have  consented,  but  counsel  for  respond-
ents  have  not  consented.  Accordingly,  leave  of this  Court
is being  sought  to  file  the  annexed  brief  amicus  curiae.

Brief  of  Jewish  Rights  Council  as  Amicus  Curiae.

6

This  brief  is  submitted  by  the  amicus  curiae  condi-
tionally  upon  the  granting  of  the  motion  for  leave  to  file
to  which  it  is  annexed.

Interest  of  the  Amicus.

The  interest  of  the  amicus  is  set  forth  in  the  motion

for  leave  to  file  to  which  this  brief  is  annexed.

'  Opinions  Below.

Some  of  the  opinions  of  the  District  Court  are  re-
ported  at  338  F.  Supp.  582  and  at  345  F.  Supp.  914.
These  opinions,  together  with  the  remainder  of  the /opin-
ions  and  orders  of  the  District  Court,  are  printed  in  the
Joint  Appendix  filed  in  this  court  by the  petitioners  with
their  petitions  for  writs  of certiorari.  The  opinion  of  the
Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Sixth  Circuit  is  reported  at
484  F.  2d  215  (advance  print)  and  also  is  printed  in  the
Joint  Appendix  (110a).

Statement  of  the  Case.

This  class  action  was  commenced  in  August  of  1970  for
the  purpose  of  desegregating  the  public  schools  within
the  City of Detroit,  Michigan.  By 1970,  the  various  public
schools  in  the  City  of  Detroit  where  racially  unbalanced,
while  the  overall  pupil  population  in  the  City  school  sys-
tem  was  63.8%  black.
The  “principle  causes”  of  such
imbalance  were  found  by the  District  Court  to  have  been
“population  movement  and  housing  patterns”  (33a)  as
well  as  “economic  factors”  (23a)  plus  the  fact  that  “blacks,
like  other  ethnic  groups  in  the  past,  have  tended  to sepa-
rate  from  the  larger  group  and  associate  together”  (34a).

The  District  Court  searched  among  the  85  separate  and
independent  suburban  school  districts  in the  three  counties

7

of Wayne,  Macomb  and  Oakland  (just  outside  the  City  of
Detroit)  to establish  a “desegregation  area”  for  the  cross-
reassignment  and  transportation  of  pupils  with  those  in
the  City  of  Detroit.  The  District  Court  found  some  53
of these  suburban  school  districts  to  be  suitable  for  this
cross-district  pupil  reassignment  Metropolitan  Plan  in
such  a  manner  that  no  school  cluster  tend  to  be  “dis-
proportionate  black  relative  to  the  tri-county  starting
point”  (69a)  and  that  “no  school,  grade  or  classroom  b[e]
substantially  disproportionate  to  the  overall  pupil  racial
However,  there  was  no  evi-
composition”  (101a-102a).
dence  taken  with  respect  to  the  original  establishment  of
the  boundaries  of  any  of  the  tri-county  school  districts
nor  whether  any  of  the  suburban  school  districts  had
“eommitted  acts  of de jure  segregation”  (59a-60a).

The  school  districts  outside  the  City  of  Detroit  were
allowed  to  intervene  as  parties  only  after  the  District
Court  had  found  that  any  “Detroit-Only”  plan  (limited  to
the  confines  of the  City  of Detroit)  would  leave  the  public
school  system  of  Detroit  as  a  whole  “perceived  as  black”
(55a).

Decision  of  the  Court  of  Appeals.

In  its  decision  (108a,  244a),  the  Court  of  Appeals  va-
eated  and  remanded  some  of  ths  District  Court’s  Rulings
and  Orders,  including  an  order  requiring  the  acquisition
of at least  295  buses;  but  the  Court  of  Appeals  let  stand
as  affirmed  both  the  District  Court’s  Ruling  that  the  De-
troit  school  system  was  “de  jure  segregated”  (38a)  and
the  District  Court’s  crucial  Conclusion  that  “relief  of
segregation  in  the  public  schools  of  the  City  of  Detroit
cannot  be  accomplished  within  the  corporate  limits  of  the
city”  (56a,  162a).

There  was  no  evidence  of  any  acts  by  the  suburban
school  districts  which  caused  de  jure  segregation.

sequently  there  was  no  legal  predicate  for  a  color-con-
scious  desegregation  plan  for  the  City  of  Detroit  which

would  include  any  of  these  suburban  schools.
fact  that,  under  any  Detroit-Only  plan  the  City  of Detroit
would  be  perceived  by  some  as  “racially  identifiable”  as
to  its  school  system  as  a  whole,  does  not  constitute  a
In  any  event,  the  social  costs
constitutional  violation.
of  cross-district  desegregation  decrees  mandate  that  any
such  decree  be supported  by clear  and. convincing  evidence
of  the  commission  of  de  jure  acts  of  segregation  by each
and  every  school  district  affected  by such  decrees,  after  4
full  opportunity  to  be  heard  on  all  issues.

ARGUMENT.

POINT  I.

The  mere  fact  that  the  whole  public  school  system
of  a  city  would  become  racially  identifiable,  under  any
desegregation  plan  limited  to  the  city’s  geographical
limits,  does  not  furnish  a  sufficient  basis  for  a  judicial
decree  of  a  metropolitan  plan  of  cross-district  public
school  pupil  re-assignments  and  transportation,  =
ticularly  in  the  absence  of  any  evidence  of  any acts
committed  by the  suburban  school  districts  causing  ra-
cial  segregation  in the  city.

As  found  by  the  District  Court  below,  there  was  no
evidence  that  any  of  the  suburban  school  districts  in  the
tri-county  region  had  committed  any  “acts  of  de jure
segregation”  (60a),  and  no  proofs  were  taken  “with  re-
spect  to  the  establishment  of  the  boundaries  of  86  public
school  districts  in  the  counties  of  Wayne,  Oakland  and

The  sole  predicate  for  the  inclusion
Macomb”  (ibid.).
of the  suburban  school  districts  which  were  selected  as
the  “desegregation  area”  was  the  fear  of  the  District
Court  that  any  Detroit-Only  plan  would  at  best  “change
a  school system  which is now Black  and White to one  that
would  be perceived  as  Black,  thereby  increasing  the  flight
of Whites  from  the  city  and  system,  thereby  increasing
the  Black  student  population”  (55a,  Finding  8;  16la).
However,  in order  that the pupils  in the  suburban  districts
be  subject  to  an  equitable  remedy,  it  is  essential  that
these  pupils  or  their  school  districts  should  have  com-
For  the  equity  power
mitted  a  constitutional  violation.
of  a  federal  court  is  limited  to  cases  in  which  a  “con-
stitational  violation”  has  been  found.  Swann  v.  Charlotte-
Mecklenburg,  402  U.  S.  1,  16.  Thus,  in  the  absence  of
a  showing  of  a  constitutional  violation  or  any  other  fed-
eral  “legal  obligations”  (Rees  v.  City  of  Watertown,  19
Wall.  107,  121)  on  the  part  of  the  suburban  school  chil-
dren,  parents,  or  districts,  there  was  no  predicate  for
a judicial  decree  to  include  them.  For  there  was  no  dual
system,  particularly  in  the  suburbs,  to  be  dismantled  as
in  Green  v.  County  School  Board,  391  U.  8S.  430,  or  in
Swann,  supra,  402  U.  S.  1;  nor  a  previous  history  of  a
dual  system  as  in  Wright  v.  City  Council  of  Emporta,
407  U.  S.  451;  nor  any  evidence  of  the  commission  of
“acts  constituting  de  jure  segregation.”  Keyes  v.  School
District  No.  1,  Denver,  Colorado,  ........  US.  3  (Re.
71-507,  Slip  Opinion  at  p.  22,  decided  June  21,  1973).

The  District  Court,  however,  condemned  any  remedy
under  which  the  Detroit  City  school  system  as  a  whole
would  “be  perceived  as  Black”  (55a).  The  Court  of  Ap-
peals  believed  that  no  remedial  decree  would  be  satisfac-
tory  which  would  leave  the  Detroit  City  school  system
“overwhelmingly  black”  (173a).  But  there  is  nothing  in-
herently  unconstitutional  in a  65%  or  an  85%  black  school
system  as  a  whole,  for  nothing  in  the  Constitution  guar-

10

antees  that a  public  school  system  as a  whole  should
contain  less  (or  more)  than  any  particular  or  arbitrary
proportion  of blacks  or whites,  Jews  or  Gentiles.  Such
an  assumption  of unconstitutionality  is itself  insulting  to
the members  of those groups,  and somehow  reads into the
phrase  “any  person”  in the  Fourteenth  Amendment's  _
Equal Protection  Clause  the concept  of a  group right, as
thongh  the Constitution  somewhere  guaranteed  to  any
group  the “equal  protection  of  the laws.”  U.  S. Const,
Amend.  XTV.

:

It  is difficult  to see how the prediction  that the whole
public  school  system  of  the  City  of  Detroit  would  be
“perceived  as  Black”  (55a,  161a)  could  possibly  create  “a
right”  (Swann,  402  U.  S. at  15)  of  the  black  (and  white)
pupils  of  the  public  schools  in  that  City  to  attend  any
schools  in  the  separate  and  independent  suburban  school
systems.
But,  only  where  “a  right  and  a  violation”
(Swann,  402  U.  S.  at  15)  have  been  proven  can  the  broad
equitable  powers  of  a  federal  district  court  be  exercised.
Yet  no  such  violation  was  shown  to  have .been  committed
by  the  suburban  schools;  therefore,  there  was  no  basis
for  the  finding  of  the  district  court  below  that.  the
suburban  schools  must  be  included  in  the  “desegregation
area.”

:

(Italics  added.)

What  is required  by fhe Constitution  is the  admission  of
all  school  children  to  the  public  schools  “of  their  com-
munity  on  a  nonsegregated  basis.”  Brown  J, 347  U.  8.
at  487.
Every  public  school  child  has
the  right  not  to  be  “denied  admission  to  schools  attended
by  white  children  under  laws  requiring  or  permitting
segregation  according  to race.”  Brown  I, 347 U.S. at 488.
The vice in Brown  I  was the assignment  of  black  and
white  pupil-neighbors  to  different  schools  “solely  on  the
basis  of  race.”  Brown  I, 347  U.  S.  at  493.  However,  in

11

the City of  Detroit  prior to the consideration  of  the
Metropolitan  Plan,  no  Detroit  school  child,  black  or  white,
had @  right to attend any public school in the suburbs.
Yet, according to the District  Court below,  some of  the
Detroit  school  children  to be selected  by “various  lotteries”
(72a)  somehow  acquired  such  a  right  merely  because  the
City’s  public  schools  would  be “perceived  as  Black”  after
any  Detroit-Only  plan.  Only  by  assuming  that  a  pre-
dominantly  black school  system  as a  whole  is somehow
inferior  can  the  Metropolitan  Plan  approach  be justified.
Brt  such  an  assumption  itself  generates  “e  feeling  of
inferiority”  (Brown  I, 347  U.  S.  at  494)  and  does  not
comport with  the  constitutional  assumption  of the  equality
of the  races,  a  presumption  so  essential  for  Equal  Pro-
tection  of  Law.

In this  country,  there  exist  many  ethnic  neighborhoods
which. tend to  persist  by  reason  of  private  choices;  and
there  are,  in  particular,  many  predominantly  black  neigh-
horhoods  which  persist  for  a  variety  of  reasons  also.
Are  all  the  neighborhood  schools  in  all  of  such  neighbor-
hoods  necessarily  violative  of the  Constitution  even  though
some  of  them  can  and  do  educationally  serve  their  non-
integrated  student  bodies  at  least  as  well  as  the  more
“integrated”  schools?

The  spectre  of  the  District  Court's  remedy  of  such  con-
torted  pupil  assignment  practices  as  “various  strip,  skip,
island,  and  non-contiguous  zoning;  various  lotteries  hased
on  combinations  of  present  school  assignments,  ge0-
graphic  location,  name,  or  hirthday”  (72a),  hardly  the
“eompact  units”  of  Brown  IT,  349  U.  S.  at  300,  all  in
order  to achieve  a  25%  black  quota  in every  school  (213a,
_n.  1, dissent.  op.)  should  make  anyone  wonder  as
192a,
to just  how  long  this  Metropolitan  Plan  can  remain  stable.
How  long  before  the  phenomenon  of  “white  flight”  (63a)

er

12

takes  over  both  from  the  public  to  the  private  schools  and
from  the  “desegregation  area”  to  the  other  school  districts
in  the  tri-county  region  which  are  outside  of  this  “de.
segregation  area,”  thereby  necessitating  an  entirely  new
plan  at  further  social  costs  to  all  of  the  affected  pupils,
parents,  and  school  districts?

The  fact  that  the  Metropolitan  remedy  is being  opposed
by  some  43  of  the  affected  school  districts  is  evidence
enough  of the  potent  private  forces  in  opposition  to  such
a  remedy.
But  it  takes  only  a  relatively  small  per.
centage  of fleeting  whites  to  push  the  percentage  of blacks
in a school  system  from  25%  to “substantially  above  40%”
at  which  point  “white  students  tend  to  disappear  en-
tirely  from  the  school  at  a  rapid  rate.”
Bradley  »v.
School  Board  of Richmond,  338  F.  Supp.  67,  194  (KE. D.
Va.,  1972),  reversed,  462  F.  2d  1058;  affirmed  by  equally
divided  court,  412  U.  S.  92  (1973).  And  other  authori-
ties  believe  that  once  the  black  population  of  a  school
“approaches  25%,  it  rapidly  will  become  100%.”  Elden,
Gary:  “Forty  Acres  and  a  Mule,”  with  Interest:  The
Constitutionality  of  Black  Capitalism,  Benign  School
Quotas  and  Other  Statutory  Racial  Classifications,  47
Journal  of  Urban  Law,  591,  609  (1969-1970).

The  suburbs  in  the  “desegregation  area”  did  not  utilize
the  suspect  classification  of  race  as  a  criterion  for  pupil
assignments  or  district  lines,  but  the  District  Court  did
so  in  selecting  the  Metropolitan  Plan  remedy’s  contours
of  the  “desegregation  area.”
However,  courts  should
minimize,  not  maximize,  the  use  of  race,  religion,  or
ethnic  background  as a criterion  for  selecting  a  remedy  in
any  case;  for  otherwise  the  judiciary  undermines  the

878 U. S.  226, 288; Plessy v.
.  S.  587,  552,  559 (dissenting  opinion  of

13

Therefore,  rather  than  the  color
Mr.  Justice  Harlan).
conscious  “broad  brush”  approach  of the  court  below,  and
in  order  to  avoid  needless  color  conscious  judicial  rem-
edies,  any  relief  “should  be  tailored  to  fit  the  particular
violation.”  Memorandum  for the  United  States  as  Amicus
Curiae  in  Richmond  School  Board  v.  Board  of  Educa-
tion, Nos.  72-549  and  7-550,  p. 26  [412  U.S.  92].  And,  since
there  was  no  finding  that  the  suburban  school  districts
in  the  “desegregation  area”  had  gerrymandered  their
boundaries  or  otherwise  committed  color  conscious  acts  of
de jure  segregation,  it  is  fundamentally  unfair  to  select
them  out  as  constituting  the  area  which  is  “substantially
proportionate  to  the  tri-county  starting  point”  (69a)  for
bearing  the  burden  of  the  “social  goal  through  the  educa-
tional  system,  by using  law  as  a lever”  (41a).

AE LEP
AOR

POINT  I.

The  social  costs  of  judicial  decrees  for  crose-district
public  school  pupil  assignments  and  transportation
mandate  that  any  such  decree  should  be  supported  by
clear  evidence  of  the  commission  of  acts  resulting  in
segregation  by  each  and  every  district  affected  by  such
decrees  after  a  full  opportunity  to  be  heard  on  all
issues.

The  dangers  of  the  tri-county  Metropolitan  remedy  ap-
proach  of  the  District  Court  below  are  compounded  by
the  realities  of  our  national  life.
The  decision  of  this
Court  in  this  case  will  not  be  confined  in  its  impact
solely  to  the  Detroit,  Michigan,  region,  but,  will  have
major  impact  upon  virtually  every  metropolitan  area
of  this  nation.
Yet,  today  all  over  America,  grave  dis-
order  increasingly  plagues  our  schools  and  make  it  im-
perative  that  teachers  easily  be  able  to  identify  pupils
and  distinguish  them  from  outsiders.

14

_  Legislators  in  both  Houses  of  Congress  have  reintro.
duced  bills  this  year  to  deal  with  the  problem  of  crime
in the  schools:  Proposed  Safe  Schools  Act  of 1973,  S. 485,
H.R.  2650.
In  support  of  this  bill,  one  of  its  sponsors,
Senator  Cranston,  pointed  out:

“In  New  York  [City],  1200  security  aides  were
added  to  the  school  staff  just  before
iv-
ing,  in  an  effort  to  curb  robberies,  fighting,  and
racial  confrontation.”  119  ee  * Ree.  (daily)  0.  12,
S.  1074,  col.  3 (January  23,  1973)  (93rd
‘ong.,  Ist
Sess. ).

A  study  of  110  different  school  districts  all  over  the  na-
tion,  by the  Senate  Subcommittee  on  Juvenile  Delinquency,
indicates  that  assaults  on  teachers  had  increased  “by  an
incredible  percent—from  25  in  1964  to  1801  in  1968.”
Senator  Gurney,  co-sponor  of  the  proposed  Safe  Schools
Act  of  1973,  119  Cong.  Ree.  (Daily)  No.  12,  S.  1072,  col.  3
(January  23,  1973).

Much  of  our  problem  of  Crime  in  the  Schools  is due  to
“outsiders—people  who  don’t  belong  in  the  schools  in  the
first  place.”  Congressman  Bingham,  House  sponsor  of
the  proposed  Safe  Schools  Act  of  1973,  118  Cong.  Ree.
(daily)  No.  29,  H  1598,  col.  2  (February  29,  1972)  (92d
Cong.,  2d  Sess.).  As  further  emphasized  by  Congress-
man  Bingham:

“We  must  at  least  in  every  school  be  able  to
spot  individuals  who  clearly  don’t  belong—and
spot  them  quickly.”
Congressman  Bingham,  118
29,  H  1598,  col.  2  (Feb-

ong.  Ree.  (daily)  No.

ruary  29,  1972)  (92d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.).

For  this  reason  alone,  it  appears  imperative  that  the  use
of  carefully  controlled  neighborhood  school  district  at-
tendance  boundaries  which  are  “compact  units”  (Brown

15

Il, 349  U.  S.  at  300)  should  not  be  rejected  out  of  hand,
but  only  after  a  full  opportunity  to  be  heard  by  all  af-
fected  school  districts.  Yet,  such  an  opportunity  was  de-
nied  to  the  suburban  school  districts  whose  subpoena
duces  tecwm  seeking  crime  statistics  in the  Detroit  schools
Joint  Appendix:
was  quashed  by  the  District  Court.
91 1a-212a  (dissenting  opinion  below).

In  balancing  the  equities  in  a  school  busing  case,
it  is  well  to  consider  the  following  findings  of  a  New
York  State  Commission:

“Members  of  the  Commission  are  convinced  that,
despite  the  apparent  failure  of  busing,  the  people
of  our  State  cannot  afford  to  lose  sight  of  the
original  mandate  of  Brown  both  in  spirit  and  in
If  busing  has  failed,  we  can  and  must
letter.
ereate  an  atmosphere  in  which  every  child  in  our
State  has  the  opportunity  for  an  equal  educa-.
a  *  **
“Tf  busing  has  failed,  that  failure  has  resulted
in  a  major  cause  of  academic  unrest;  the  chasms
continue  to  grow;  the  fears  continue  to  multiply;
the  incidents  grow  more  numerous;  the  violence
grows  more  violent  *
*  *

“Tn  order  to  put  the  busing  question  in  its
proper  perspective,  we  must  again  bear  in  mind
that  busing  in  rural  areas  has  long  been  in  use
and  accepted,  and  that  the  busing  which  appears
to  have  failed  is  the  busing  solely  for  the  purpose
of  integration.

“Busing  for  the  purpose  of  integration  in  most
eases  takes  youngsters  from  their  own  neighbor-
hoods  and  customs  and  daily  way  of  life.  They
are  forced  to  a the  entire  school  day,  a  good
part  of  their  life,  in  an  alien  and  strange  atmos-
phere  and  way  of  life  which,  if  only  for  that  rea-
son  would  appear  hostile  to  the  child.  *  *  *

“There  is,  however,  little  apparent  confusion
among  the  majority  of  those  interviewed  regarding
what  they  feel  to  be  the  solution.  To  them,  equal
opportunity  and  quality  education  should  be  pro-

16

vided  to  the  student  irrespective  of  the  commu.
nity  in  which  he  lives,  together  with  voluntary  ex.
panded  enrollment  rather  than  compulsory  bus-
ing.”  Third  Report  of  the  Temporary  [New  York
State]  Commission
To  Study  The  Causes  Of
Campus  Unrest  (“Academy  or  Battleground”),
Part  I  (“Secondary  Schools”),  Section  II  “See.
ondary  Schools  1971-1972—Movement  of  Students
to  Achieve  Integration,  Alternative  Busing  Pro.
posal”),  pages  35-36,  45  (Albany,  N.  Y.,  1972).
See  also:  Zebra  v.  Pittsburgh  School  District,  41 L. W"
2167  (Penna.  Common  Pleas,  Allegheny  County,  Septem.
ber  14,  1972),  where  a  desegregation  plan  led  to  such  vio.
lence  that  an  injunction  was  issued  against  the  continued
mandatory  attendance  at  schools  by  the  transferred  stu-
ents.  And  as  found  in  the  Report  of  the  Committee  on
the  District  of  Columbia  with  respect  to  the  experience
with  busing:

“The  reason  for  this  bussing  was  the  so-called
‘Skelly  Wright’  decision  where  the  Court  ordered
the  Public  School  System  to  achieve  racial  balance
and  to  equalize  pupil  costs.
In  either  case  these
efforts  have  not  been  successful.”
Investigation
And  Study  of  the  Public  School  System  of  the
District  of  Columbia,  House  Report  No.  91-1681
(91st  Cong.,  2d Sess.),  at  p. 84.

In  any  event,  in  view  of  the  physical  problems  and
social  costs  of  busing  in  our  nation’s  public  schools,  it
seems  most  imperative  that  the  federal  judiciary  should
not  interfere  with  the  day-to-day  operation  of these  schools
unless  “there  is  a  clear  case  of  constitutional  infringe-
ment”  (Esteban  v.  Central  Missouri  State  College,  415  F.
2d  1077,  1090  [8  Cir.  1969,  per  Blackmun,  J.])  and  with
&  minimum  of  interference  when  there  is  such  a  clear
case.  For  it has  been  well  stated  that:

17

“The  efforts  to  disestablish  segregated  schools
have  repercussions  far  beyond  the  original  confines
of  the  problem  and  into  broader  issues  such as
flight  from  urban  areas,  the  decay  of  our  cities,
and  the  ability  of  our  governmental  structures  to
survive  the  overall  economic  consequences.”  United
States  v.  Texas  Education  Agency,  467  F.  2d  848,
889  (5  Cir.,  en  banc,  1972).

And  the  Metropolitan  remedy  threatens  to  cause  even
further  repercussions  in  the  suburban  areas  of  our
country.

In  the  face  of  the  further  disciplinary  and  educational
problems  inherent  in  the  cross-district  Metropolitan  rem-
edy,  it  is  well  to  note  that  there  is  increasing  evidence
that  the  supposed  educational  benefit  of  racial  integra-
tion  to  black  children  is  being  seriously  questioned  by the
scholars.  It now  appears  that  even  the  cumulative  effects
of complete  racial  balancing  in  public  schools  can  close
the  educational  gap  between  blacks  and  whites  by  only
20 to 30 percent,  if at all.  See:  Christopher  Jencks,  “Bus-
ing—The  Supreme  Court  Goes  North,”  The  N.  Y.  Times
Magazine,  November  19,  1972,  page  41  at  page  120,  col.
1; Thomas  F.  Pettigrew,  et  al.,  “Busing:  A  Review  of
‘The  Evidence,”  The  Public  Interest,  No.  30  (Winter,
1973),  page  88  at  page  99;  David  J.  Armor,  “The  Evi-
dence  on  Busing,”  The  Public  Interest,  No.  28  (Summer,
1972),  page  90  at  page  99;  Frederick  Mosteller  and Daniel
P.  Moynihan,  On  Equality  of  Educational  Opportunity,
“A  Pathbreaking  Report,”  page  3 at  page  24  (Library  of
Congress  Catalogue  No.  78-117653;  Random  House,  1972) ;
David  K.  Cohen,  Thomas  F.  Pettigrew,  and  Robert  T.
Riley,  “Race  and  the  Outcome  of  Schooling,”  Id.  page
343  at  page  358.

it appears  that  differences  between  schools account

Black  plaintiffs  in  class  actions  involving  potential  bus-
ing  orders  now  have  learned  to  compromise  such  cases,
with  district  court  approval,  by  settling  for  little  or  no
integration  to  remedy  racial  imbalances,  in  exchange  for
a  quota  of  “top  administrative  jobs”  for  black  teachers.
Nordheimer:  “Atlantic  N.A.A.C.P.  Defies  National  Of-
fice  and  Refuses  to  Drop  Approval  of  School  Plan.”  N.  Y.
Times,  July  11,  1973,  at  page  19.  Thus  busing  decrees
And  it
lend  themselves  to  the  pressures  of  corruption.
should  be  noted  that  the  District  Court  in  the  present
case  at  bar  found  that  an  “affirmative  program”  (76a)
of  hiring  and  promoting  black  school  faculty  and  staff
would  be  needed,  thus  lending  itself  to  compromise  with
respect  to  the  busing  aspect  of  any  final  decree.

eines

 ——

19
In  view  of  the  questionable  educational  benefits,  the
potential  for  disruption  and  economic  consequence,  and
the  evils  of  quotas  involved  in  busing  decrees,  there
appears  to  be  no  equity  in  busing  for  the  purpose  of
securing  racial  balances  in the  public  schools.  Therefore,
the  District  Court,  in  its  “balancing”  of  the  equities,
abused  its  discretion  in  selecting  the  drastic  color  con-
scious  remedy  of  a  Metropolitan  busing  remedy  in  the
it  of  the  questionable  benefits  flowing  from  such
decrees,  and  in  the  face  of  the  unquestionable  detriments.

eae

Conclusion.
The  judgment  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  should  be  af-
firmed  except  insofar  as  it affirmed  the  Rulings,  Findings,
and  Conelusions  of the  District  Court,  and  in  this  respect
it should  be  reversed  and  the  cause  should  be  remanded
to the  District  Court  with  directions  to  dismiss  the  com-
plaint  with  respect  to  the  school  districts  outside  the
City  of  Detroit.

Respectfully  submitted,

The interest  of The School  Town  of Speedway,  Indiana ani
The School City of Beech Grove, Indiana,  arises from the follow.
ing facts.  Both  movants  are school  corporations  created  ani
existing  under Indiana  law.  ty: howhead gar uberis.
tems  which  serve,  respectively,
civil  town  of Speedway  ani
the  civil  city  of Beech  Grove,  Indiana,  two  small  communities

by

On  August  18,  1971,  the  United  States  District  Court  for the
Southern  District of Indiana, after a trial of  an action brought
the United  States,  entered  a  judgment  in  which  it found that
the  Indianapolis  Public  School  System  (IPS)  was  guilty of seg-
regatory  practices  in the operation  of its schools.’  The triif
court  speculated  in  its opinion  that  a desegregation  plan limited
to IPS would  not remain  demographically
stable  and that IPS
would  at  some  future  point  have enrolled  a  higher  percentag
of black  students  than  was acceptable  to the district court.

Subsequently  an intervening  complaint  was filed and nineteen
school  corporations  and certain  state  officials  added  as defend-
ants.  Movants  were  among  the added  school  corporations.
Following  another  trial,  the court entered  a  judgment  in which
it found,
infer alia:  (1)  that the prior judgment  against  IPS
was res judicata  against  the added school  corporations  and state
officials;  (2)  that  none  of the added school  corporations  wer
guilty  of segregatory
practices;  and (3) that all of the school
corporation  defendants  were amenable  to orders  effecting st-
dent transfers between such defendants  in quantities designed
1.  U.S.  v.  Bd. of School  Commissioners,  332  F  —

aff'd.,  474  F.  2d  81  (7th  Cir.  1973),  cert.  den.  37 L.
1041  (1973).

er ee ee ee

3

to achieve  a  prescribed  degree  of racial  balance  within  IPS.
That decision  is presently  on appeal  to the United States  Court
of Appeals
for the Seventh  Circuit.  Thus,  movants  are  in-
volved in an action similar  to the case at  bar.  A  decision by
this Court in the instant  action  may  — precedent  for  the
Seventh  Circuit’s  decision.

Both the School  Town of Speedway,  Indiana  and The School
City of Beech  Grove,  Indiana  are  independent  school  corpora-
tions  which  have  territorial  boundaries  coterminous  with  the
municipalities  which  they serve.  As such  entities,  they have com-
munity interests  distinct  and  separate  from  other  types  of school
corporations  which  will  be  adversely  affected  if this  Court  ap-
proves  the power  of district  courts  to enter  orders  such  as  those
made in the cases  at bar.  The distinct  interests  of school  corpora-
tions serving  towns  and  cities  has  not  heretofore  been  argued  by
the parties in this appeal  or considered  in the Court of Appeals
below,  and  such  interests  are  relevant  to  any  disposition  of this
appeal.  Movants  do not  believe  that  the  arguments  made  in the
attached  Brief will  be made  by any other party  to these appeals.

Respectfully  submitted,

INTEREST  OF AMICI  CURIAE.

‘The  interest  of The  School  Town  of Speedway,  Indiana  and
The  School  City  of  Beech  Grove,  Indiana,  arises  from  the
following  facts.  Both  movants  are  school  corporations  created
and existing  under  Indiana  law.  They  own  and  operate  school
which  serve,  respectively,  the  civil  town  of  Speedway
and  the  civil  city  of  Beech  Grove,  Indiana,  two  small  com-
munities  adjacent  to  the  City of Indianapolis.
On  August  18,  1971,  the  United  States  District  Court  for
the Southern  District  of Indiana,  entered  a  judgment  in which
it found  that  the  Indianapolis  Public  School  System  (IPS)  was
guilty of segregatory  practices  in the  operation  of its  schools.’
The  trial  court  speculated  in  its  opinion  that  a  desegregation
plan limited  to  IPS  would  not  remain  demographically  stable
and that IPS would  at some  future  point have  enrolled  a higher
percentage of black  students  than  was  acceptable  to the district
Subsequently  an  intervening  complaint  was  filed  and  nine-
teen  school  corporations  and  certain  state  officials  added  as
defendants.  Movants  were  among  the  added  school  corpora-
tions.  Following  another  trial,  the  court  entered  a judgment  in
which  it found,  inter  alia:  (1)  that  the  prior  judgment  against
IPS was  res  judicata  against the  added  school  corporations  and
state  officials;  (2)  that none  of the  added  school  corporations
were  guilty  of  segregatory  practices;  and  (3)  that  all  of  the
school  corporation  defendants  were  amenable  to  orders  effect-
ing student  transfers  between  such  defendants  in  quantities
designed to achieve  a prescribed  degree of racial  balance  within
IPS.  That  decision  is presently  on  appeal  to  the United  States  -
1.  U.S.  v.  Bd.  of School  Commissioners,  332  F.  oe
affd.,  474  F.  2d  81  (7th  Cir.  1973),  cert.  den.  37  L.
1041  (1973).

2d

2

Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Seventh  Circuit.2  Thus,  movants  ar
involved  in an  action  similar  to  the  case  at  bar.  A decision  by
this  Court  in  the  instant  action  may  provide  precedent  for th
Seventh  Circuit’s  decision.

ARGUMENT.

“It  is not  the  time  to  try  to  say  with  final  authority
what  the  town  has  meant  in  American  life.  Its  mean-
ings are  profound  and  various.  But  of its  importance
there  can  be  no  question.”*

One  of  the  questions  in  the  case  at  bar  and  in  the  action  in
which  these  amici  are  parties  is whether  a  federal  district  court
may  order  the  busing  of  school  students  between  separate,  in-
dependent  school  corporations  in order  to remedy discriminatory
practices:  found  to  exist  in  only  one  of  such  school  corpora-
tions.  These  amici  believe  that  the  far  reaching  impact  of such
an.  order  may  be  seen  in  clearer  detail  when  viewed  from  the
vantage  point  of  a  school  corporation  which  serves  a city or
town  separate  and  distinct  from  the  school  district  in  which
such  discriminatory  practices  were  effected.

Throughout  this  country,  hundreds  of  school  corporations
exist  which  have  geographical  boundaries  coextensive  with  small
Several  of  such  school  corporations  are
towns  and  cities.

2.

United  States,  Plaintiff-Appellant  and  Donny  Brurell  Buckley,
et  al.,  Intervening  Plaintiffs-Appellees  vy.  Board  of  School  Com-
missioners,  et  al.,  Nos.  73-1968  through  73-1984,  in  the  Seventh
Circuit  Court  of  Appeals.

3.

P.  Smith,  As  a  City  Upon  a  Hill,  The  Town  in  American

History  (Alfred  A.  Knopf,  1966),  p.  307.

4.

In  1972,  the  U.  S.  Bureau  of  the  Census  reported  the
existence  in  the  United  States  of  15,281  “independent  school  dis-
tricts,”  i.e.,  school  districts  which  are  administratively  and  fiscally
independent  of  any  other  government.  Of  this  number,  597  of
such  districts  had  coterminous  “citywide”  boundaries.  The  same
source  reported  a total  of 1457  “dependent  school  districts”  of which
247  had  coterminous  “citywide”  boundaries.  In  total,  there  were,
in  1972,  844  “citywide”  systems  with  8.2  million  pupils.  U.  &.
Dept.  of  Commerce,  Bureau  of  the  Census,  Vol.  1,  Governmenial
Organization,  1972  Census  of Governments,  pp.  3, 6,  8 and  40.

3

to  these  appeals.  These  amici  are  also  school  corpora-
tions  which  have  such  territorial  boundaries  and  which  serve
small  municipalities  located  in  the  State  of  Indiana.

Typical  of  such  municipal  school  corporations  is  one  of
these  amici,  The  School  Town  of Speedway.  The  civil  town  of
way,  Indiana,  which  it serves,  has  a population  of approxi-
mately  17,500.  The  town  is governed  by  a  board  of  trustees
elected  by the  citizens  of  the  town.  The  town  board  is  em-
to  control  all  other  municipal  departments  and  ap-
Schools  which  serve  the  com-
points  their  administrators.
munity are  owned  and  administered  by an  independent  corpora-
tion created  by statute  and  designated  as  The  School  Town  of
Speedway.  The  administration  of  that  school  corporation  is
vested  in  a  three-man  board  of  school  trustees  appointed  by
the civil  town  board  of  trustees  on  a  non-partisan  basis.  The
School  Town  of  Speedway  neither  owns  nor  operates  school
buses.  All  schools  are  physically  located  within  the  town
boundaries  so  that  students  have  access  thereto  either  by walk-
ing or  other  means  of  transportation  provided  by the  students
or  their  parents.  The  operational  funds  for  the  schools  are
provided  almost  entirely  through  taxes  paid  by citizens  of  the
Although  this  small  community  is  geographically
Town.
dwarfed  by the  adjacent  City  of  Indianapolis,  it encompasses
large  industries  in  which  many  of  its  citizens  work.  In  short,
it is a  distinct  community  whose  citizens  take  pride  in  local
community  projects  and. operations,  and  which  has  a  municipal
government  and  school  system  responsive  to  the  local  problems
and needs  of the  community.

The  rationale  of state  legislatures  in fixing  school  boundaries
to the boundaries  of the small  towns  and  cities  which  they serve
is not  limited  to  mere  physical  convenience:

[If  we  except  the  family  and  the  church,  the  basic
form  of social  organization  experienced  by the  vast  major-
jty  of  Americans  up  to  the  early  decades  of  the  20th
Century  was the small  town.  In the words  of  Thorstein
Veblen:  “The  country  town  is one  of the  great  American

4

institutions;  perhaps  the  greatest,  in  the  sense  that  it ha
and  had  and  continues  to  have  a  greater  part  than  any
other  in shaping  public sentiment  and  giving  character  tp
American  culture.”®

The  real  essence  of a  community  is found  in the  fact  that it

has  a  center,  and  the  beginning  of  a  community  arrives  when
its  members  have  a  common  relation  to  the  center.°  It is this
common  relationship  which  gives  vitality  to  a  school  system
serving  the  small  community.  The  importance  of  this  common
relationship  becomes  readily  apparent  when  one  views  the chaos
of  many  big  city  school  systems  as  contrasted  to  the  stability
and  quality  of those  found  in smaller  communities.

The  small  community,  then,  as  many  legislatures  and  educa.
tors  have  found,  provides  a  desirable  environment  for  imple-
mentation  of  a  community-wide  school  system  which  can  truly
give  consideration  to  such  important  factors  as  home-school
communication,  children  attending  school  within  the  vicinity
of home,  minimization  of transportation  safety hazards,  economy
of  cost  and  ease  of  pupil  placement  and  administration.  See
opinion  of Mr.  Justice  Powell,  concurring  in  part  and  dissent-
ing in  part,  Keyes  v.  School  District  No.  1, Denver,  Colorado,
i UL  S. 5  37  L. Ed.  2d  548  (1973);  Deal  v.  Cincinnati
Bd.  of Ed.,  369  F.  2d  55  (6th  Cir.  1966),  cert.  den.,  389
U.  S.  847  (1967).

It is thus  beyond  argument  that  towns  and  cities  provide  a
logical,  reasonable  and  desirable  setting  for  operation  of in-
dependent  school  corporations.  The  essential  question  is whether
federal  courts  can  forcibly  transfer  children  attending  such  a
school  system,  and  otherwise  disregard  the  independent  nature
of  such  a system,"  in order to effect  a  judicially  prescribed
degree  of racial  balance  in a  given  geographical  area.

5.  P.  Smith,  supra,  pp.  vii-viii.
6.  Will  H

Ed.:  The  Writings  of  Martin  Buber  (New
ks;  1956),  p.  129.

7.  In one of the district  court  orders  entered  in the case in
which  these  amici  were
the trial court has presumed the
power t0 comeolidate all of the sthool corporations  in the Indlanagilt

ies,

,

ee

5

state act which  contravenes a
Absent  an overt,  affirmative
Constitutionally  protected  right,  federal  courts  have  no  power
to circumscribe  the rights of the states  to establish  municipalities
and school  corporations  to serve  the members  of  such  com-
munities.  Spencer  v.  Kugler,  326  F.  Supp.  1235  (D.  N.  J.
1971),  aff'd.  per curiam,  404  U.  S.  1027  (1972);  Gomillion  v.
364  U.  S.  339  (1960);  and  Hunter  v.  Pittsburgh,
207 U. $.  161  (1907).  These  principles  alone,  in  addition  to
this Court’s  rejection  of the  concept  of “racial  balance  or  mix-
ing”, Swann  V.  Charlotte-Mecklenburg  Bd.  of Ed.,  402  U.  S.
1 at 24  (1971),  provide  sufficient  authority  for  a  reversal.  of
the lower courts  in  the  instant  case.

In an  attempt  to  avoid  the  limitations  found  in  the  above
precedents  and  other  cases,  the  Sixth  Circuit  Court  of Appeals
in the cases  at bar  apparently  adopted  the  theory  that  all school
cotporations  are agents of  the state and since the state is
responsible  for  school  matters,  the  rights  of  individual  school
corporations  could  be  ignored.  Such  a theory  is flawed  in many
respects.  These  amici  wish  to only point out  that  if this concept
is not rejected  by this Court,  it will  allow one school corporation
to be held  to  answer  for  the  wrongdoings  of  another.  In  ad-
dition  to  the  fact  that  guilt  by association  is anathema  to  our
jurisprudence,  such  a  novel  concept  would  effect  a fundamental
deprivation  of  the  Constitutional  rights  of  the  innocent  school
corporation.  Long  ago this Court  held  that  private  corporations
created  under  state  law  for  school  purposes  were  entitled  to  the
protection  afforded  by  the  guarantees  of  the  United  States
-  Constitution.  Pierce  v. Society  of Sisters,  268  U.S.  510  (1925).
No  reason  exists  for  holding  that  public  school  corporations
are  to be treated  differently.

Many other  legal arguments  could be discussed,  but they will
undoubtedly  be made by the parties to these appeals.  It is the
tal  Memorandum  of
area “along  me
Decision,  entered  mber 6, 1973,  by The U. S.  Distr t Court,

tan  lines”.  Su

6

practical  and factual  impact  of the opinion  of the Sixth Cin
in the instant case which these amici wish to primarily  emply
size.  If that  opinion  is allowed  to stand  and becomes  the ly
of the land, judicial  power  in desegregation  cases  will know »
bounds.  Students  will be transported  from the small  towns ay
cities  in which  they, their families  and friends  reside,  into ty
schools  of large metropolitan
of busing fy
areas.  The burden
racial  balance  may fall upon school  children  who have alway
walked  to a  nearby  school  (as is the case with most Speedway
students).  Parental  interest  and  participation
in school  affaix
will be frustrated.  Many school  officials  are elected  by votes
who reside  within  the territorial  boundaries  of the school  cx.
poration  or  district.“  Small  town  citizens  may  thus  experienc
the anomaly  of being  required  to send their children  to a schoo
system  administered  by  officials  over  whom  they  exercise  »
voting  control.  Every  school  corporation  located
in any degre
of proximity  to a large metropolitan  area will become  amenable
to remedial  racial  balancing  decrees,  no matter  how separate o
independent  those  school  corporations  may  be.  The  touchston
of judicial  power  will  be  demography,  not  equity.

_——

CONCLUSION.

The district courts involved in the cases with which this Brief
js concerned  have  promulgated  decrees  which eventually  could
destroy one of the traditional  fabrics of American life. Their
decrees  formulate  a  blueprint  for  federally  constituted  school
systems which ignore.the  natural  community  interests
of parents,
teachers  and children.  Our nation  has drawn its life from small
communities,  and  it  is  itself  a  community  of  communities.°
This Court should not affirm a  court decree which would forever
destroy the rights of the citizens of  the towns and cities of this
nation  to educate  their  children  in school  corporations  designed
and established  to  serve  individual  communities.
Respectfully  submitted,

1

OPINION  BELOW

The  opinion  below,  filed  by the  United  States  Court  of
Appeals  for  the  Sixth  Cirenit  (hereafter  Sixth  Circuit)  ig
reported  as  Bradley  v.  Milliken,  (6th  Cir.,  1973)  484  Fda
215  (Certiorari  Joint  Appendix  pp.  110a-240a)  (hereafter
cert.  app.).

JURISDICTION

The  United  States  Supreme  Court  has  jurisdiction  to
review  this  case  by writ  of certiorari  pursuant  to 28 U.S.C.
§ 1254(1),  and  has  accepted  it for  such  purposes  by grant-
ing  said  writ  on  November  19,  1973.

CONSENT  OF  PARTIES

This  amicus  brief  by the  State  of  Indiana  is  filed  pur-
suant  to Rule  42 of the  Rules  of the United  States  Supreme
Court  and consent  of the parties  is not required  pursuant  to
Rule 42(4).

}

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

L

Whether,  in  a  school  desegregation  case  involving  a
metropolitan  area  in which  one  school  system  has been
found  to be de jure segregated  and  all other  districts  found
not  to  be  de  jure  segregated,  a  federal  district  court  can
enter  orders  segarding  the  transfer  or  exchange  of pupils,
against  those  other  school  systems  or  districts  which  are
geographically  close  to the  segregated  system  when  there
is no finding  against  those  school  systems  or districts,  no.
finding  that they were formed  as a  part of a  state sup-
ported de jure segregated  system,  and no finding that they
exist  in  order  to  perpetuate  such  a  system,  when  those: ee

_ ——e

ecders have the effect of developing massive busing and
student  transfer  programs  among  the  various  districts
“2  gnd  which  were  entered  solely  to  establish  a  court-
acceptable  “desegregation”?  plan  in  the  one  segregated

1.
Whether the state can be compelled  to entirely reorga-
sine local school districts in metropolitan  areas within the
state in order  to remove  only black children from one  school
system  and  only  white  children  from  another  school  sys-
tem and exchange  them between  systems  when  only  one
school system was found to be illegally segregated, when
there  were  no  findings  against  any  other  school  system
and when  the  only  alleged  ‘‘act’’  of the  ‘State’?  was  pur-
ported  to  have  been  committed  entirely  within  the  ille-
gally  segregated  system,  but  which  in  fact  had  no  causal
connection  whatever  upon  racial  percentages  or  numbers
in any  school  system.

OF  THE  AMICUS  OCURIAE
INTEREST

The  State  of  Indiana  stbniits  its brief  amicus  curias
sinee this case involves  similar  questions  of law to a case
arising  out of Indiana  which is currently  on appeal  to the
United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Seventh  Cireuit
(hereafter  Seventh  Circuit).  U.S.A.  éd Buckley  et  al. »,
Board  of School Commissioners  of the City of Indianapolis,
et  al.,  (No.  73-1968  through  73-1984)  (hereafter  Indianap.
olis).  In that case neither  the State  of Indiana  nor  the In.
diana  General  Assembly  were  named  parties.  However,
named  as  added  defendants in  the court  below—the  United
States  District  Court  for  the  Southern  District  of Indiana
(hereafter  District  Court)—were  Otis  R.  Bowen,  as  Gov-
ernor of the State of  Indiana,  Theodore  L. Sendak,  as At-
torney General  of the State  of Indiana,  Harold  H. Negley,
as Superintendent  of Public  Instruction  of the State of In-
diana,  and the  Indiana  State  Board  of Education,  a public
corporate  body  (hereafter  State  Defendants).  The  State
Defendants  were  added to the lawsuit  after  the District
Court  had  made  a  finding  of  de  jure  segregation  on  the
part  of the  Indianapolis  Public  School  System  (hereafter
IPS),  332 F. Supp.  655,  (1971),  and which  was  properly on
appeal  to the  Seventh  Circuit  at the  time  the  State  Defend-
ants  and  nineteen  school  districts  were  added  as
defendants.

The  Indiana  case,  like  the  case  at  bir, is an  extremely
complex  piece  of  school  desegregation  litigation  involving
the transfer  or  exchange  of pupils from IPS  which has been

county,  city,  and  town  boundaries  to  twenty-three  other
school  districts in  eight  (8) counties  found  noé  to be guilty
of any de jure violations.

t

‘The  State  of Indiana,  pursuant.  to its  Constitution,
Article 8, Section 1 (1852),  and  statutes  duly  enacted,  has
provided  for  a  system  of  common  schools  wherein  tuition
~shall: be without  charge  and  ‘‘equally  open  to all.’”  The  ef-
fectuation  of those  provisions  has  always  been  in  the  de-
velopment  and control of the local schools in the State of
Indiana,  since  school  systems  are  created  locally,  controlled
oeally,  and  are  primarily  financed  locally  (by issuance  of
their own school  bonds  and  the  taxation  of local  property).
The function  of the  State  agencies  in education,  such  as  the
Office of the  Superintendent  of Public  Instruction  and  the
Indiana  State  Board  of  Education,  is  a  serviee  function
designed  to  assist  the  local  schools  in  their  various  indi-
vidual programs.

In  the  Indianapolis  Standard  Metropolitan  Statistical
Area  (hereafter  ISMSA)  there  are  44  independent  school
systems  with  a  total  1972  enrollment  of  approximately
261,482  school  children,  Twenty-one  (21)  of  those  school
are  now  involved  in litigation  in the  District  Court
8
and in the  Seventh  Circuit  in  a  case  which  is  similar  to
this case.  The  enrollment  of  those  twenty-one  (21)  school
systems for 1972 was  approximately  205,175  school children.
(attached  appendix  following  p.  A-63).

The  disposition  of  the  Detroit  case  in  the  Sixth  Cirenit
has caused a very  serious  threat  to the  continued  existence
of the school  systems  in the  ISMSA,  many  of  which have
existed  in  their  present  or  predecessor  form  since  before
the Civil War, and as early as 1838.
“The primary  interest  of  the  amicus  is in explaining  to
this Court how the Detroit  case affects  those Indiana  school
systems,  and  why  the  Sixth  Circuit  in  the  decision  below
was incorrect  and should be reversed.

The purpose  of  the amicus is also to suggest to this Court
that the decisioh  now  presented for review  is of a signifi-

cance  which  equals  that  of Brown  v.  Board  of Education,
347  U.S.  483  (1954).  The  result  here  can  have  the  effect of
placing  almost  the  whole  of  metropolitan  development  in
the  United  States  under  a  federal  judicial  superin-
tendency,  and  such  a  case  previously  has  not  been  before
this.  Court.  This  effect  has  staggering  implications,  as  is
evident  from  examining  the attached  United  States  Bureau
of  Census  Charts  of  urbanized  areas in Indiana,  Illinois
and  Michigan  from  the  1970  census  (attached  appendix
following  p.  A-63).

The  amicus  brief  is limited  in its  primary  discussion  to
that part of the  Court  of Appeals  opinion which  permits  the
development  of a ‘‘ Metropolitan  Area Desegregation  Plan,’
484  F.2d  215,  250  Cert.  App.  at page  173a,  for  the  Detroit
Metropolitan  area.

In  this  case,  unlike  Keyes  v.  School  District  No.  1, 8
S.Ct.  2686  (1973),  there  are  multi-school  districts  involved,
against  which  no  finding  of racial  discrimination  has  been
entered,  but  like  Keyes,  there  was a finding  of discrimina-
tion in  one  school  district.  Unlike  Bradley  v.  School  Board
of Richmond,  Virginia,  462  F.2d  1058  (4th Cir.  1972),  aff'd,
93  S.Ct.  1952  (1973),  there  is no  history  of racial  discrimi-
nation  in  the  additional  school  district  defendant-inter-
venors,  and  no  finding  that  the  out-of-Detroit  City schools
are  or  ever  have  been  anything  other  than  integrated
School  systems.  Similar  to Richmond  is  the percentage  of
blacks  in the city school  systems,  with the Detroit  School
City  being  about  64%  black,  and: Richmond  about  70%
black.  Also,  as in Richmond,  the Detroit  school  city can
desegregate  now and eliminate  racially  identifiable  schools.

and wm white.

9

-  This case  commenced  as  a  school  desegregation  case
the Detroit  City School  system,  and the district
eourt wishes  to end it  as a  case which alters  the racial
imbalance  between  that school system and the added de-
fendant-intervenors.  In  that  way  it is much  like  the  Indi-
anapolis  case  (see attached  Appendix,  pp.  11 and  12),  and
much  like  Spencer  v.  Kugler,  326  F.Supp.  1235,  (D.N.J.
1971), aff’d,  404  U.S.  1027  (1972), in  which  this  Court  re-
jected  an  attack  made  upon  the  racial  imbalance  found  in
New Jersey school  systems.

The essential  fact in this  case  is not  that  the  Detroit  City
schools  were  segregated,  according  to the  district  court,  but
that the  added  school  systems  were  not  found  to  be  segre-
gated.  Those  school  systems  are  to  carry  the  judicial  bur-
den,  They  were  not  heard,  were  not  tried,  and  were  not
at trial; no  evidence  was offered  against  them, and
present
no  findings  were  made  against  them.  Nevertheless,  they
are the subject  of the district  court’s  orders in  Detroit.

The  critical  factual  difference  between  this  case  and

that  finding  to  the  Seventh  Circuit,  the  added  school  de-
and the State  Defendants  were  brought
into court

in a second  lawsuit  (see  attached  appendix  p.  A-6)  by a

Indiana
The Indianapolis  case  commenced  on  May 31, 1968,  when
the  United  States  of  America  filed..a  Complaint  in  the  >
District  Court  which  was  assigned  cause  number  IP-68-C-
995. The action  by the United  States  was  brought  pursuant
to 42  U.S.C.  § 2000c-6(a)  and  (b),  and  was  tried  by  the
Court  on  July  12-21,  1971,  Defendants  in  the  aforemen-
tioned complaint  were  The  Board  of School  Commissioners
of the City of Indianapolis,  Indiana,  its  Superintendent  of
Schools,  and  members  of  its  Board.
“On August  18, 1971,  the  Court  issued  its ‘Memorandum
of Decision’’  permanently  enjoining  defendants,  their  suc-
cessors  in office,  officers,  agents,  employees  and  all  those
in active  concert  or  participation  with  them  from  ‘‘dis-
criminating  on  the  basis  of  race  in  the  operation  of  the
Indianapolis  School  System,’’  and  further  ordered  the
defendants  to take  seven  steps  to ‘‘fulfill  their  affirmative
duty to achieve  a nondiscriminatory  school  system.’  332 F.
Supp.  655,  680.  Affirmed  474  F.2d  81  (7th  Cir.,  1973).
Cert. denied  407  U.S.  920, 93  S.Ct.  3066  (1973).
_  A part of the  District  Court’s  findings  in the first  Indi-
anapolis case was that that school board constructed  three
high school buildings  in 1961, 1963, and 1967, the placement
of which constituted  acts  of de jure segregation.

On July  20, 1973  the District  Court issued  its ‘‘Memo-
randum  of  Decision.’’  See  attached  Appendix,  p.  A-1.  Ih
that Decision  the Court  found acts of de jure segregation
on  the part of the Indianapolis  Public  Schools  in the place-
ment  of three  (3)  high  schools,  and that those  acts were
imputed  to the Indiana  State  Board  of  Education  and the
State  Superintendent  of  Public  Instruction.  However,  the
Court  found  that  there  were  no  de jure  acts  of segregation
attributable  to  the  added  defendant  school  districts.  The
District  Court  also  found  that  the  desegregation  of the  In-
dianapolis  Public  School  System  cannot  be  accomplished
with  its own  boundaries  primarily  because  of the  possibility
of resegregation  ‘‘within  a  matter  of two  or  three  years.”

The  District  Court  in the Indianapolis  case  stated  that
it  was possible  to desegregate  IPS within  its own boun-
daries,  stating  at page 7 of  its Decision:

that as a sheer exer-
cise in mathematics,  it would be possible  for this Court
