5

BRIEF  FOR AMICUS  CURIAE
MEXICAN  AMERICAN  LEGAL  DEFENSE  AND
EDUCATIONAL  FUND

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

The  interest  of  amicus  curiae  is  set  forth  in  the

foregoing  motion.

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT

Children  of  metropolitan  Detroit  are  denied  equal
educational  opportunity  because  their  schools  are
racially  segregated.  Since  it is clear  that  state  action
has  been  at  the  root  of  Detroit’s  current  segregation,
this  Court’s  instructions  in  Keyes  v.  School  District
No.  1, 93  S.Ct.  2686  (1973)  should  control  the  instant
ease;  that  is,  it  is  an  issue  of  fact  whether  or  not
Detroit  is  “separate,  identifiable,  and  unrelated”  to
the  remainder  of  metropolitan  Detroit.  Detroit  is  an
integral  part of the Detroit  metropolitan  area  in which
race  is  the  only  evident  line  of  demarcation.

Segregation  in  Detroit  has  its  genesis  in  the  state:
Michigan  creates  its  school  districts,  and  exercises
great  control  over  local  school  boards.  The  state  has
both  acquiesced  in  segregative  policies  and  taken  an
active  affirmative  role  in  nullifying  integration  ef-
forts.  School  districts  including  more  than  one  politi-
eal  unit  are  legion  in  Michigan  while  consolidation  is
commonplace.

While  there  is

ample evidence  of  State  segregatory
action  from  which  intent  may  be inferred  as  in Keyes,

the  intent  of  the  state  authorities  is  not  pivotal  be-
cause  state-created  school  district  lines  in  Detroit
have  a  racially  segregative  effect.  It  is  evident  that
there  is  no  compelling  state  interest  for  maintaining
Detroit’s  racially  exclusive  boundary  lines,  because
these  lines  were  not  sacrosanct  in the  past  when  they
were  crossed  to  further  segregation  and _ because
boundaries  have  been  abolished  for  a  multitude  of
other  educational  reasons.

The  state  cannot  now  use  school  district  boundaries
to limit  a district  court’s  remedial  powers  where  cross-
district  student  assignments  are  necessary  to  achieve
an  effective  and  unitary  school  system.  District  courts
are  bound  to  consider  only  remedies  that  will  achieve
a unitary  school  system.  Only plans that  work  in prae-
tice are  constitutionally  permissible.  To  that  end,  dis-
trict  courts  have  a wide  latitude  of tools  available,  but
these  must be used to achieve  a  unitary  system.  What-
ever  may  be  the  limitations  of  a  compelling  state  in-
terest,  these  are  certainly  not  transgressed  where  the
state  itself  has  consistently  crossed  district  lines.

This  Court  has  unwaivingly  required  lower  courts
to  measure  plans  against  a  standard  of  effectiveness.
The  lower  court  has  followed  the  admonitions  of  this
Court  in  selecting  the  plan  it felt  would  be  most  ef-
fective;  anything  short  of  a  metropolitan  plan  would
be  futile  and  resegregative.

7

ARGUMENT

This  desegregation  suit  presents  questions  concern-
ing the  equitable  powers  of the  federal  courts  to  give
full  and  meaningful  relief  against  the  continued  ef-
fects  of  de  jure  school  segregation,  when  that  relief
requires  crossing  lines  drawn  by the  state  government
responsible  for  the  segregation.  In this  case,  the  peti-
tioners  were  ordered  by  the  District  Court  below  to
prepare  a  desegregation  plan  which  would  not  be  lim-
ited, in scope  or  effect,  to the  inner  city schools  of the
metropolitan  Detroit  area  but  which  would  encompass
schools  located  throughout  the  Detroit  metropolitan
area  for  the  purpose  of creating  a  unitary  integrated
school  system.  In  reaching  that  decision,  the  District
Court  found  that  system  wide  segregation  in  Detroit
city schools  was  the  result  of unconstitutional  acts  by
the  State  and  that  ho  desegregation  plan  limited  to
Detroit  could  create @ unitary  integrated  school  sys-
tem.  The  Court  of Appeals  affirmed.  Since  no  specific
remedy  was  ordered  by the  courts  below,  the precise
question  to  be  determined  is  limited:  May  a  federal
district  court  order  consideration  of  a  desegregation
plan  involving  cross-district
student  assignments,
within  a  single  metropolitan  area  in  a  single  state,
when  the  court  has  found  that  unconstitutional  segre-
gation exists  in the inner  city schools  of the metropoli-
tan  area,  and  that  the  relief  involving  those  schools
alone  can  remedy  the  unlawful  segregation.  In  this
brief  we  take  the  position  that  the  decisions  of courts
below  were  eminently  sound—indeed  they  were  man-
dated  by the  principles  contained  in  decisions  of this
Court—and  that  they should  be affirmed.

7

I;

THE  OHILDREN  OF  METROPOLITAN  DETROIT  ARE  DENIED
EQUAL  EDUCATIONAL  OPPORTUNITY  BECAUSE  THEIR
SCHOOLS  ARE  RACIALLY  SEGREGATED.

.

“‘Separate  educational  facilities  are  inherently  un-
equal.”  Brown  v.  Board  of Education,  347  U.S.  483,
.  gener-
495  (1954).  “To  separate  [black  students]  .
ates  a  feeling  of  inferiority  as  to  their  status  in  the
community  that  may  affect  their  hearts  and  minds  in a
way  unlikely  ever  to  be  undone,  .
.  Segregation
with  the  sanction  of law,  therefore,  has  a  tendency  to
[retard]  the  education  and  mental  development.  of
Negro  children  and  to  deprive  them  of  some  of  the
benefits  they  would  receive  in  a  racial[{ly]  integrated
school  system.”  Id.,  at. 494.

.

We  submit  that  at the heart  of the  Brown  principle
is what  Edmond  Cahn  once  labeled  as  the  ‘‘consumer
perspective,”  Cahn,  Law  in the  Consumer  Perspective,
112  U.  Pa.  L,  Rev.  1  (1963);  Cahn,  Tue  Prepica-
MENT  OF  Democratic  Man  (1961).  The  “consumer
perspective”  simply  insists  that  the  most  valid  judg-
-ment  which  can  be  given  about  institutions  is  given
by those  people  whom  they  are  intended  to  serve—
i.e.,  the  ‘‘consumer”  of  that  institution’s  services;  in
this  case,  minority  students  of  segregated  schools.

If  schools  are  identifiably  ‘“‘minority”  or  ‘‘white,”
if they are  more  than  ‘‘just  schools,”  Green  v.  County
School  Board,  391  U.S.  430,  442  (1968),  the  injury
so  zealously  to  be  guarded  against  under  Brown
oceurs.  The  indentifiability  of  schools,  together  with
the  resultant  injury  occasioned  by them,  is largely  a
matter  of  a  child’s  perception.  If  a child  can  say

9

(or  see  or  feel)  ‘‘This  is  one  of  Detroit’s  minority
schools—and  this  is  one  of  Detroit’s  Anglo  schools,”
the  school  is,  within  the  meaning  of  Brown  and  its
progeny  a  segregated  school.

Detroit

Given  the  factual  context  of  this  case,  a  child  at-
tending  any  metropolitan
school  knows
whether  his or  her school  is an  Anglo  one  or  a minority
one.  He  or  she  also  probably  lives  within  a  half  hour
of the  center  of  the  city.  Hither  his  or  her  parents
or  his  or  her  neighbor’s  parents  work  within  the  city
limits.  No  matter  where  he  lives  in  the  metropolitan
area,  the  child  may  go  to  a  hospital  in  the  center  of
the  city  if he  is  ill,  and  his  parents  are  likely  to  go
into  the  city  center  for  entertainment.  The  child  will
learn  about  his  world  from  the  metropdlitan  news-
papers,  television  and  radio,  none  of  which  limit
themselves  to “Detroit  only”  or  “suburban  only”  news
-coverage.  There  are  few  definite  natural  geographical
boundary  lines  which  divide  up  the  city,  and  what
lines  exist  are  casually  crossed  by metropolitan  areas
citizens  daily.  The  cohesiveness  of  the  metropolitan
area  is further  evidenced  by the  state’s  creation  of the
Metropolitan  Detroit  Water  System,  the  Metropolitan
Sewage  System,  the  Huron-Clinton  Metropolitan  Port
Authority)  and  its  creation  of  intermediate  school  —
districts  which  provide  educational  services  on  an
interdistrict  basis  within  the  metropolitan  area.

In  Keyes v. School  District  No.  1,  93  S.Ct.  2686
(1973)  this  Court  was  asked  to  consider  whether  a
district  court  erred  in  requiring  evidence  of  segrega-
tion  in  every  part  of  a  city  in  order  to prove  a  case

10

of  district-wide  segregation.  This  Court  emphatically
rejected  the  lower  court’s  standard,  and  reversed
holding  that  where  there  is  evidence  of  segregation
in  a  city,  the  burden  then  shifts  to  the  defendants  to
show  that  the  remainder  of the  city is not  tainted  by
the infection.  93  S.Ct.  at 2697.  It is nearly  a  tautology
to  say  that  if  one  area  of  a  city  is  black  the  other
areas  must  be  racially  affected  in that  they  are  more
white.  See  93  S.Ct.  at  2695.  On  remand  the  lower
court  considered  evidence  of inter  alia,  transportation
patterns,  water  services,  and  sewage  services  in  order
to  determine  whether  the  Park  Hill  area  of  Denver
was  geographically  separate  from  the  remainder  of
Denver.  In  painstakingly  following  this  Court’s  in-
structions,
Judge  Doyle  found  that  Park  Hill  was
not  a  self-sustaining  enclave  but  was  integrally  re-
lated  to Denver  as  a whole:

.

.  [P]laintiff’s  evidence  established  that  the
.
Park  Hill  area  is  not  treated  differently  from
the  surrounding  areas  with  respect  to  the  pro-
vision  of  fire  and  police  protection,  water  sup-
plies  and  sewage.

Keyes  v.  School  District  No.  1, No.  C-1499  (D.  Col,
Dee.  11,  1973).

This  Court  chafed  at  the  thought  of  a  “separate,
identifiable,  and  unrelated”  portion  of a  district  and
commented  that  such  phenomena  “must  be  rare.”  93
S.Ct.  at  2695.  This  Court  further  held  that  “such  a
determination  is  essentially  a  question  of  fact  to  be
resolved  by the trial  court  in the first  instance  ..  .”
93  S.Ct.  at  2695.  Similarly,  the  trier.  of  fact  in  the

aa

1l

instant  case  has  established  that  the  City  of  Detroit
is not a “separate,  identifiable,  and  unrelated”  portion
of the  Detroit  metropolitan  area.  The  respondents  in
the  instant  case  have  sought  to  remove  the  state
imposed  artificial  political  barriers  to  a  unitary
system.
The only clear  physical  demarcation  between  various
parts  of  the  metropolitan  area  is  that  schools  for
people  living  near  the  center  of  the  city  are  pre-
dominantly  minority  and  schools  for  people  living
further  away  are  predominantly  Anglo;  or,  to  put
it in  terms  of  the  child’s  perspective—people  living
near  the  center  attend  “minority”  schools,  and  people
living  further  away  attend  “Anglo”  schools.

|

The  population  of  the  metropolitan  Detroit  area
is approximately  25%  minority  and  75%  Anglo.  If  a
child  were  in a school  with  a  similar  proportion  of
minority  and  Anglo  students,  he  would  not  perceive
it as “minority”  or “Anglo”  but as  reflecting  the metro-
politan  Detroit  society  in  which  he  lives.  Thus,  if we
assume  that  only  schools  with  student  bodies  of  15-
40%  minority  would  reflect  Detroit  society  and  thus
would  not  be  identifiable  by the  child  as  an  “Anglo”
school  or  a  “minority”  school,  most  of  the  schools  in
the metropolitan  Detroit  area  are  identifiable  by them
as  “minority” or “Anglo.”  The  social  cohesiveness  of
the metropolitan  area  and  physical  pronnaniy further
assure  such  identification.

Welokey  hdiniak Gry Wehiad dik an kre dah Bo doen
to be a  “minority”  or  “Anglo,”  it is obvious  that  the
schools  are  segregated.  That  this  segregation  is caused

12

by  the  state’s  maintenance  of  evanescent  boundaries
does  not  alter  the  child’s  perception,  his  injury  or  the
segregation.  For almost  two decades  this Court has
recognized  that  “Separate  educational  facilities  are
inherently  unequal.”  Brown  v.  Board  of Education,
supra,  347  U.S.  at  495.

II

THE  STATE  OF  MICHIGAN  HAS  DENIED  PLAINTIFFS  THE
OF THE  LAWS
EQUAL  PROTECTION

A.  The  State  Has  Created  the Segregated  School  System.

The  state  defendants  do  not  challenge  the  lower
court’s  finding  that the present  segregation  in Detroit
public  schools  resulted  from  such  practices  of  the
Detroit  Board  of  Education  as:  segregative  zoning
and  assignment  practices,  formulation  and  modifica-
tion  of  attendance  zones  to  perpetuate  segregation,
segregative  transportation  of students  (both by busing
minority:  students  to minority  schools  often  past avail-
able  Anglo  schools  and  by  following  “intact  trans-
portation”  plans  which  resulted  in all minority  classes
in  predominantly  Anglo  schools),  maintenance  of
“optional  attendance”  zones  and  “school  feeder”  plans
that  perpetuated  or  exacerbated  racial  segregation,
and  segregative  school  construction  policies  which
resulted  in  most  new  schools  opening  as  one-race
schools.  These findings  are amply  supported  by the
record,  as  evidenced  by the  state’s  failure  to challenge
them,  and  are  sufficient  to  establish  the  duty  to  de-

,

13

m

the dual  school  system  and to convert  to a
segregate
unitary  system.  See  the  Circuit  Courts  findings  as  to
-  these  practices  in the Appendix  at 118a-15a.  Cf., Keyes
v.  School  Dist.  No.  1,  93  S.Ct.  2686  (1973);  Swann
v.  Charlotte-Mecklenburg  Brd.  of Educ.,  402  U.S.  1
(1971);  Green  v,  School  Board,  391  U.S.  430  (1968).
)
The  state  defendants  challenge  the  lower  court’s
power  to  remedy  the  unconsfitutional  segregation  by
utilization  of  a  desegregation  plan  involving  cross-
district  student  assignment.  These  petitioners  seek  to
insulate  themselves  from  the  remedial  power  of  the
federal  court  by  denying  that  any  party  save  the
Detroit  Board  of  Education  is  responsible  for  the
unconstitutional  segregation  and  by  suggesting  that
any remedial  order  must  therefore  be limited  in scope
to affect
within  the school
only  those  schools  located
district  administered  by the Detroit  school  board.  Both
assertions  ate erroneous.

To  suggest,  as  do  the state  defendants,  that  they
are  not  legally  responsible  for  the  segregation  in  .
Detroit,  and  that  the  scope  ofthe  remedy  in this  case
must  be  limited  in  effect  to  Detroit  ignores  the  per-
vasive  control  that  the  state  has,  and  exercises,  over
education  in  Michigan,  and  moreover,  ignores  the
state’s  segregatory  intent  which  is  evidenced  by  the
state’s  exercises  of  that  power  in the past.

State  law  creates  and  defines  the  boundaries  of
tities  and  counties.  Such  boundaries  are  often  useful
for the  administration  of many  matters,  but  they need
not  coincide  with  school  division  -boundaries,  In  fact,
there  are  a  number  of  school  divisions  which  include

14

several  political  units  in Michigan,  and  Michigan  law
permits  the State  Board  to consolidate  school  districts
even  if  consolidation  overlaps  such  political  lines,
Indeed  in  Michigan,  from  1964  until  1968,  700
Michigan  school  districts  were  consolidated  for  all
sorts  of  educational  and financial  reasons,  many  by
direct  order  of the  State  School  Board  without  regard
to  local  school  board  desire  or  approval.  See  Circuit
Court  opinion,  Appendix  at  167a-69a.  Metropolitan
school  districts  overlapping  political  lines  are  not  un-
common  elsewhere.  For example,  the City of Charlotte,
North  Carolina,  and  the  County  of Mecklenburg  com-
prise  a  single  school  system.  See  Swann  v.  Char-
lotte-Mecklenburg  Brd.  of Educ.,  402  U.S.  1  (1971).

The  State  of  Michigan  has  permitted  interdistrict
school  attendance  plans in the past and  it  exercises
a  pervasive  control  over the affairs  of the local school
boards,  both  directly,  e.g.,  by requiring  state  approval
of  textbooks,  and  indirectly,  ¢.g.,  by  exercising  a
power  of  review  and  veto  over  local  board  decisions.
See  Circuit  Court  opinion,  Appndix  at  165a-72a.
Of  course,  many  decisions  are  delegated  to  the  local
boards,  for  administrative  convenience.  To  the  extent
that  the State  has  delegated  to local  officials  the  power
to  determine  the  boundaries  of  the  administrative
units  for  schools,  the  exercise  of those  powers  is still
the  State’s  responsibility  and  they  must  be  exercised
in  conformity  with  the  State’s  constitutional  obliga-
tions.  Regardless  of  the  legal  mechanism,  the  result
of  that  exercise  of  power  in  this  case  is  clear—
segregated  schools.  Having  created  a segregated  school

—_—

:

15

system  and having  refused  to exercise  its power  in a
manner  that  would  desegregate  it, the  State  of Michi-
gan  has  violated  its  constitutional  duty  under  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.

The  instances  of  the  unconstitutional  acts  of  the
Detroit  School  board  are  obviously  chargeable  to  the
State  Board  of Education,  which  controls  the  Detroit
Board.  Indeed,  the  very  school  construction  policies
which  were  found  to exacerbate  segregation  in Detroit
schools  were  subject  to  the  approval  of  the  State
Board.  The  state  board  acknowledged  this  control
when  it published  its  1966  Joint  Policy  Statement  and
the  School  Plant  Planning  Handbook  containing  an
express  admonition  to  local  boards  to  consider  racial
balance  as  one  of the  factors  in  selecting  new  school
sites  or  constructing  schools.  (PX  174,  Va.  13.)  Peti-
tioners  seek  to  avoid  this  obvious  conclusion  by  sug-
gesting  that  local  school  boards  have  plenary  power
to  assign  pupils  and  design  attendance  zones  within
their  districts,  and  that  the  state  has  no  responsibility
for  the  results  of  these  actions.  The  record  bears  no
support  for  such  an  assertion.

What  the  record  evidences,  with  respect  to  the
metropolitan  Detroit  area,  is  that  the  state  afforded
local  boards  great  power  to  draw  attendance  zones
and  student  assignment  plans,  but  only  for  so  long
as  those  powers  were  exercised  in  a  segregatory
manner.  Thus,  in  April,  1970,  the  Detroit  school
board  adopted  a  modest  plan  to  effect  desegregation
in  Detroit.  Three  months  later,  the  State  legislature
enacted  Public  Law  48  which  served  to  “impede,

ae

16

delay  and  minimize  racial  integration  in  Detroit
schools”  (District  Court  opinion,  Appendix  at  28a)
by effectively  nullifying  the  plan of the  Detroit  school
board.  This  state  power  over  local  school  board  de-
cisions,  of  course,  has been  recognized,  by Michigan
law,  to inhere  in the state,  as the  Circuit  Court
properly  concluded  in  its  opinion  below:

.

.
.  Michigan  has  always  regarded  education  as
fundamental  business  of  the  State  as  a  whole.
Local  school  districts  are  creatures  of  the  State
and  act  as  instrumentalities  of  the  State  under
State  control.

Cireuit  Court  opinion,  Appendix  at  167a  (citations
omitted).

.

Petitioners  suggest  that  the  action  of  the  state  in
enacting  Public  Law  48  is  irrelevant  here  because  it
“had  no  causal  connection  with  the  distribution  of
pupils.”  (Petitioners’  Brief  in No.  73-434  at 41.)  This
misses  the  point  of  the  proof.  The  state  has  asserted
that  lack  of  proof  of  its  affirmative  involvement  in
past  decisions  of  the  local  board,  that  have  been
found  to  have  led  to  segregation,  coupled  with  its
delegation  of  certain  powers  to  local  board  defeats
any  attempt  to  place  responsibility  for  resulting  seg-
regation  on  it.  But  enactment  of  Public  Law  48
effectively  undermines  the  state’s  position.

of the state
It  may be true  that  the  local  agents
were  often  permitted  to  reach  their  own  decisions
without  state  interference.  But  the  reason  for  leaving
them  alone  was  not  lack  of  power  in  the  supervisory
agency,  or  lack  of  knowledge  as  to  the  probable

3a

:

17

segregatory  effects  of  those  actions.  Rather,  past
acquiesence  is only  explicable,  given  the  state’s  actions
in enacting  Public  Law  48, because  the state  approved,
and  desired,  the  resulting  segregation.  As was said
of  the  Denver  school  board,  and  as  is  no  less  the
case  here with  respect  to the past acts  of  the state
of Michigan:  “A  finding  of  international  segregation
in  one  portion  of  a  school  system  is  highly  -relevant
to  the  issue  of  the  board’s  interest  with  respect  to
the  other  segregated  schools  in  the  system.”  Keyes,
supra,  93  §.Ct.  at  2697.  The  courts  below  properly
weighed  this  evidence  in  concluding  that  the  state,  as
well  as  the  city  was  legally  responsible  for  the  seg-
regation  in  Detroit  schools.

The  question  in  this  case,  therefore,  is not  whether
state  action  has  resulted  in a  segregated  school  system
—that  clearly  has  occurred.  Rather,  the  question  is,
having  acted  in  such  a  manner  as  to  segregate  the
races  in  fact,  can  the  state  demonstrate  justification
of a sufficiently  compelling  nature  to excuse  the  effects
of  its  actions?  If  such  justification  does  not  exist,
then  the  school  system  is segregated  within  the  mean-
ing of  Brown  and  the  equity  powers  of the  District
Court  below  were  properly  applied  to  we  the
resultant  segregation.

B.  The  School  Boundary  Lines  Are Unconstitutional
Because
They  Operate  to Discriminate  on the Basis of Race  and Are
Not Justified  by a  Compelling  State Interest.

Sometimes  there  are  no  obvious  criteria  for  draw-
ing  lines,  yet  it  is  still  useful  to  draw  a  line.
lines  in the
This  is true  of  the school  boundary

18

Detroit  area.  There  are  no  natural  geographical
boundaries  dividing  the  area,  and  the  lines  which  are
imposed  are  flexible  and  subject  tc  constant  change
through  consolidation  proceedings.  These  lines  are
drawn  to  facilitate  various  political  and  social  fune-
tions.  But  the  criteria  on  which  the  lines  are  based
are  arbitrary.  Such  arbitrary  lines  are  properly
viewed  with  suspicion  when  their  effect  is  to  dis-
advantage  blacks  and  Latinos.  Thus,  courts  have  not
permitted  school  attendance  lines  to be  drawn  around
a  residentially  segregated  neighborhood  when  other
attendance  zones  are  just as  feasible.  Brewer  v.  School
Board  of City of Norfolk,  397  F.2d 37  (4th  Cir.  1968).
Even  attendance  zones  which  follow  natural  or  his-
torical  boundaries  have  been  struck  down  when  they
operate  to separate  the  races.  See  Henry  v.  Clarksdale
Municipal  Separate  School  District,  409  F.2d  682  (5th
Cir.),  cert.  denied,  396  U.S.  940  (1969)  ; United  States
v.  Greenwood  Municipal  Separate  School  District,  406
F.2d  1086  (5th Cir.),  cert.  denied,  395  U.S.  907  (1969) ;
Clark  v.  Board  of Education  of Little  Rock,  426  F.2d
1035  (8th  Cir.  1970);  and  Monroe  v.  Bd.  of Comm’rs
of City  of Jackson,  Tenn.,  427  F.2d  1005  (6th  Cir.
1970).  See  also  Davis  v.  Board  of  School  Commis-
stoners,  402  U.S.  33  (1971).

Although  the  foregoing  is  adequate  evidence  of  |

state  action  from  which  intent  may  be  inferred  as  in
Keyes,  if the  effect  of districting  is racial  segregation
and  the  justification  is weak,  there  is no  need  to probe
the  psychological  motivation  of  the  legislature  to
determine  whether  there  was  in fact  a conscious  racial

aly

19

purpose.  Anyone  involved  in such a classification  will
readily  perceive  that  they  are  treated  unequally,  and
courts  will  agree  with  that  perception.  See  Jackson  v.
Godwin,  400  F.2d  529  (5th  Cir.  1968);  Hawkins  v.
Town  of Shaw,  437  F.2d  1286  (5th  Cir.  1971).

This  Court  has  construed  statutes  prohibiting  racial
discrimination  in  voting  and  employment  from  this
perspective.  See  Gaston  County  v.  United  States,  395
U.S.  285  (1969).  In  Griggs  v.  Duke  Power  Co.,  401
U.S.  424  (1971),  an  employer  used  a  standardized
test  for  hiring  and  promotion  decisions.  The  district
court  found  there  was  no  racial  purpose  or  invidious
intent  in adopting  the  test  although  the  Anglos  tested
generally  did  better  than  blacks;  by  a  divided  de-
cision  the  Fourth  Cireuit  Court  of  Appeals  affirmed.
Nevertheless,  this  Court  found  that  the  use  of  the
test  violated  Title  VII  of  the  Civil  Rights  Act  of
1964  which  made  it  unlawful  for  an  employer  “to
limit,  segregate,  or  classify  his  employees  in  any  way
which  would  deprive  .
.
.  any  individual  of employ-
ment  opportunities  .
.
.  because  of  such  individual’s
race.”  The  Court  ruled  that  an  employment  test  could
be used  only  if it was  in  fact  related  to  performance
of  the  job,  and  that  the  employer’s  motive  in  using
the  test  was  not  the  significant  question.

The  issue  in  cases  of  racial  discrimination  thus  at
least  becomes  whether  the  classification  or  boundary
line  that  operates  to  discriminate  against  minorities
is justified  by a  compelling  non-racial  state  interest.
See  eg.,  Loving  v.  Virginia,  388  U.S.  1, 9  (1967) ;
McLaughlin  v.  Florida,  379  U.S,  184  (1964).

20 :

In  the  instant  case,  when  the  effect  of  districting
is  considered,  and  when  Michigan’s  professed  “justi-
fications”  are  weighed  to  determine  their  compelling
nature,  it  becomes  clear  that  ordering  cross-district
education  of  students  within  these  school  districts  is
not  beyond  the  remedial  powers  of  a  federal  district
court.  Whatever  the  State  of  Michigan’s  interest  in
maintaining  students  in  schools  located  only  within
the  school  boundary  lines  of  the  place  of  residence,
it  has  no  compelling  interest  in  utilizing  those  lines
for  school  administrative  zones  which  result  in  seg-
regated  schools.

First,  the  State  ignored  boundary  lines  to  imple-
ment  its  earlier  policy  of  segregation.  During  this
period,  many  Michigan  children  were  bu».l  across
school  district  lines  because  the nearest  school  of their
race  was  located  in  another  district  as  in  the  case  of
the  Carver  school  children.  See  Circuit  Court  opinion,
Appendix  at  152a.

_

Seeond,  school  boundary  lines  have  often  been
ignored  in  the  past  to  further  other  educational  pur-
poses.  From  1964  to  1968,  the  number  of  Michigan
school  districts  decreased  from  1438  to  738,  pursuant
to  a  statutory  scheme  designed  to  require  all  districts
to  operate  as  K  through  12  systems.  Other  examples
include  consolidation  for  financial  purposes.

The  historic  practice  of  combining  several  political
subdivisions  into  one  school  division  and  in consolidat-
ing  school  districts  for  diverse  reasons  demonstrates
that  there  is no  compelling  state  interest  in  maintain-
ing  separate  school  districts  and  that  the  State  of

21

disparities

Michigan  has  not  in  the  past  considered  the  reasons
offered  in  this  case  to  be  “compelling.”  The  state
asserts,  aS  an  additional  reason,  the  state’s  interest  in
“local  control”  over  schools,  citing  as  authority,  this
Court’s  decision  in  San  Antonio  ISD  v.  Rodriguez,
411  U.S.  1  (1973),  which  held  a  state’s  interest  in
local  control  of  education  to  be  sufficient  to  justify
in  financing.  Of  course,
interdistrict
Rodriguez  is  inapposite  since  it  did  not  involve  a
racial  classification.  Hence,  proof  of “rational”,  rather
than  “compelling”  justification  over  the  disparities  in
Rodriquez  was  deemed  sufficient.  But  this  case  is  a
ease  involving  a  racial  classification.  Indeed,  Rodri-
guez  is  inapplicable  for  yet  another  reason.  The
interest  in  local  control  asserted  by the  state  here  is
incredible  given  the  record  of  state  control  over  edu-
cation.  Cf.  Circuit  Court  Opinion,  Appendix  at
167a.  Indeed,  this  principle  of  ‘“‘local  control”  ap-
parently  was  not  deemed  peremptory  when  the  state
overruled  the  Detroit  school  board’s  desegregation
action  in  1970.  Moreover,  until  details  of  the  metro-
politan  plan  are  formulated  and  approved  it  is
difficult  to  assess  its  effect  on  local  control,  since
many  variants  of  cross-district  attendance  plans  are
compatible  with  local  control.  One  thing  is  clear,
however:  since  there  is no compelling  reason  to  main-
tain  these  school  boundary  lines  which  separate
children  by  race,  they  should  be  struck  down.

22

C.  The  State  Cannot  Use School  District  Boundaries  to Limit a
District  Court’s  Remedial  Powers  Where  Cross-District
Student  Assignments  Are  Necessary  to  Achieve  an  Effective
and  Unitary  School  System.

It  is difficult  to  discuss  the  appropriateness  of  the
contemplated  remedy  in  this  case  because  no  specific
plan  has  yet  been  approved.  Discussion  must,  per-
force,  be limited  to a general  proposition,  i.e., whether,
a  district  court  may  consider  a  metropolitan  desegre-
gation  plan.  In discussing  the  problem,  it is important
to  focus  on  what  is not  in  issue  as  well  as  what  is in
issue.  First,  no  specifics  as  to  costs,  transportation
time  or  distances,  number  of  students  involved,  ete.,
can  be  discussed  because  these  factors  are  not  yet
known.  In  fairness,  it  must  be  assumed  that  the
District  Court  will  reject  any  plan  which  involves
excessive  additional  costs,  or,  if all  plans  do so,  that  it
is  still  possible  that  no  metropolitan  plan  can  be,  or
will  be,  ordered.  We  are  left  then  with  the  following
proposition  for  discussion:  May  a  district  court  con-
sider  a  desegregation  plan  involving  cross-district
student  assignment  at  reasonable  cost  of  time  and
money,  in  order  to  desegregate  a  dual  system,  if  the
only  alternative  to  such  cross-district  assignments  is
implementation  of  a  remedy  which  can  be  expected  —
to  result  in  an  artificially  bounded  all-black  school
system  surrounded  by equally  artificially  bounded  all-
white  school  systems?  We  argue  below  that  the  lower
courts  were  correct  in concluding  that  a  district  court
may  consider  such  a  remedy.

Ever  since  the  1968  decision  in  Green  v.

School
Board,  391  U.S.  480  (1968),  clarified  the  duties  im-

23

posed  on  school  boards,  there  has  been  no  doubt  as
to  the  goal  to  be  achieved  in  desegregating  a  dual
school  system.  The  goal’ was,  and  remains,  to  convert
to  a  “unitary  school  system.”  And  where  a  school
system  fails  to  achieve  this  goal,  it  is  within  the
remedial  power  of  federal  courts  to  order  implemen-
tation  of  plans  designed  to  achieve  it.

.

In  defining  the  scope  of  the  remedial  powers  of  a
district  court  and  in  seeking  guidance  as  to  the
proper  and  necessary  exercise  of  that  power  “.  .
.
words  are  poor  instruments  to  convey  the  basic  fair-
ness  inherent  in  equity.  Substance,  not  semantics,
must  govern  .
.”.  Swann,  supra,  402  U.S.  at  26
(1971).  No  definition  of a “unitary  school  system”  will
suffice  to  govern  all  the  conceivable  factual  patterns
presented  by the,  literally,  hundred  of  school  cases
decided  annually.  No  “per  se”  rules  can  be  fashioned
which  will  serve  as  a  panacea  for  every  conceivable
problem  that  may  arise  in  every  case.  Despite  these
difficulties,  however,  some  requirements  of  a  pupil
desegregation  plan  are  quite  clear,  objective  and  un-
ambiguous.  Against  these,  as  a  minimum,  any  plan
must  be  measured  for  sufficiency.

1, The Goal.

The  most  important  consideration  for  any  plan  is
the  goal  to  be  achieved—a  “unitary  school  system”.
Any  plan  which  ‘is  calculated  or  can  be  predicted  to
fail  in achieving  that  goal  is  beyond  constitutional
redemption,  whatever  savings  of  costs  and  inconyeni-
ence  it  may  achieve.  A  plan,  at  a  minimum  nmuust-

)

24

“promise  to work  realistically  to  convert  promptly  to
a system  without  a ‘white’  school  and  a system  without
a  ‘negro’  school,  but  just  schools.”  Green,  supra,  391
U.S.  at  442.  Or,  as  this  Court  rephrased  the  same
goal  in  Swann:  “.  .
.  the  district  judge  or  school
authorities  should  make  every  effort  to  achieve  the
greatest  possible  degree  of  actual  desegregation...  .”
Swann,  supra,  402  U.S.  at  26.

Especially  to  be  noted  in  the  last  passage  is  the
emphasis  on  the word  “possible.”  Obviously,  possibility
is  a  function  of  the  tools  available.  What  then  are
the  tools  or  methods  available  to  courts  in  achieving
“the  greatest  possible  degree  of actual  desegregation”?

[|
|

2.  Methods  Available  to Achieve  Unitary  Status.

-

Although  references  exist  elsewhere,  this  Court
spoke  most  fully  about  the  methods  to  be  used  in
achieving  desegregation  in  Swann.  Little  can  be
added  to  the  Court’s  description  of  those  methods:

a.  attendance  zones—‘‘a  frank—and  sometimes
drastice—gerrymander  of  school  districts  and
_  attendance  zones”  resulting  in  zones  “neither
compact  nor  contiguous;  indeed  they  may  be
in  the  opposite  ends  of  the  city.”  Id.,  at  27.

b.  racial  attendance  criteria—‘pairing;  cluster-
ing;  or  ‘grouping’  of  schools  with  attendance
assignments  made  deliberately  to  accomplish
the  transfer  of Negro  students  out  of formerly
segregated  Negro  schools  and  transfers  of
white  students  to  formerly  all-Negro  schools.”
Id.,  at  27.

25

e. transportation—“Desegregation  plans  can  not
be  limited  to  the  walk-in  school.”  Id.,  at  30.

d.  administrative  convenience—“But  all  things
are not  equal  in a  system  that  has been  de-
liberately  constructed  and  maintained  to  en-
force  racial  segregation.  The  remedy  for  such
segregation  may  be  administratively  awkward,
inconvenient  and  even  bizarre  in  some  situa-
tions  and  may  impose  burdens  on  some...
.”
Id.,  at  28.

Any plan  must  consider  all  these  available  methods,
in light  of the  goal  to be achieved.  Failure  to  consider
these  methods  or  failure  to  utilize  them  or  refusal  to
accept  one  of  two  alternatives  because  one  does  and
the  other  does  not  utilize  them,  except  where  the
choice  is  dictated  by a  compelling  state  interest  is  a
clear  failure  to discharge  the  constitutional  obligation.
The availability  of “more  promising  courses  of action”
to dismantle  a  dual  school  system  “at  the  least  places
a heavy  burden  upon  the  board  to  explain  its  prefer-
ence  for  an  apparently  less  effective  method.”  Green,
391  U.S.  at  430;  Wright  v.  Council  of City  of Em-
poria,  407  U.S.  451,  467  (1972).  If  the  failure  is
on  the  part  of  the  district  court,  it  would  require
remand  for  their  consideration  and  utilization.  See,
e.g.,  Adams  v.  School  Dist.  No.  5, 444  F.2d  99  (4th
Cir.  1971).  In  short,  within  the  context  of  the  goal
to  be  achieved,  and  in  light  of  all  the  methods  of
achieving  it available  to the  district  court,  the  court’s
discretion  is  limited  to  exploration  of  all  techniques

and  utilization  of those  which  best  achieve
that goal,
absent  the limitation  of  a  compelling  state  interest;
But  the  discretion  the  Court  vests  in  the  distric¢
court  goes only to the question  of how integration
shall  be  achieved—the  details  of  the  remedial
plan  (such  as  what  schools  shall  be paired  for the
transportation  program).  The  lower  court  has no
discretion  to alter  or  disregard  the central  re
medial  obligation—  achieving  the  greatest  degree
of school  desegregation—and  the  plan it approves
will  be measured  by that  stringent  standard.

Fiss,  The  Charlotte-Mecklenberg  case,  38  U.  Chic.  I,
Rey.  697,  702  (1971).

Indeed,  Professor  Fiss’s  analysis  best  explains the
result  in  Davis  v.  Board  of  School  Commissioners,
402  U.S.  33.  (1971),  a  companion  case  to  Swann
There,  this  Court  rejected  a  plan  that  had  bee
adopted,  but  which  allowed  a  residue  of  segregation,
Elimination  of  that  residue  required  assignment  of
students  across  major  highways,  which,  to the distrie
court  and the Fifth  Circuit  presented  a  sufficient
practical  barrier  to constitute  a “compelling”  justifica-
tion  for  the  board’s  failure  to  achieve  greater  deseg-
regation.  The  case  was  remanded  for  consideration
of the use  of bus transportation  and  split  zoning...  .

of Compelling  State Interests.
4  Limitations

|

There  are,  of  course,  limits  on utilizing  these
methods  to achieve  a  unitary  school  system—as,  in-
deed,  there  may be times  when  a proper exercise  of
diseretion  would  counsel  foregoing  their  use.  But
these  limits  and  their application  must  be  tested  by
the traditional  “compelling  state  interest”  test  com-
mon  to  racial  discrimination  cases.  Cf.,  Loving  v.
Virginia,  388  U.S.  1 (1967);  McLaughlin  v. Florida,
379 U.S. 184 (1964).  Again, Swann itself is the best
source for consideration

of these limitations.

For example,  in discussing  the use of transporta-
tion, the Court noted that busing  is now used ex-

28

»X

tensively  and  that  busing  can  not  be  considera
undesirable  simply  because  the  destination  is  ap
integrated  rather  than a  segregated  school.  It is only
when  the  state  establishes  a compelling  interest  in not
utilizing  a  particular  proposed  transportation  plan
that  it ought  to be rejected.  Or, restating  the  proposi-
tion  in  the  Court’s  language,  when  the  state  proves
that  a  proposed  busing  plan  would  “either  risk  the
health  of children  or  ‘significantly
impinge  on the
educational  process”  Swann,  swpra,  402  U.S.  at 30-31,
wong gchar  ari  Ngee  as Comes
Court.

The  Ss  sieatlina interest”  doctrine  may,  to  some
extent,  itself  be  ambiguous.  It  is clear,  however,  that
it  requires  a  good  deal  more  showing  than  does  the
traditional  equal  protection  test  of  “reasonableness”
or  “balance  of  the  hardships”  that  is  in  ordinary
equal  protection  cases.  See,  Shapiro  v.  Thompson,
394  U.S.  618  (1969)  for  discussions  of  the  proper
standards,  in both the majority  and dissenting  opin-
ions.  See  also,  Cipriano  v.  City  of Houma,  39  US,
701  (1969) ; San Antonio  Ind, Sch. Dist.  v.  Rodrigues,
93 §.Ct, 1278  (1973)  and the dissenting
opinion  of
Neck City
Judge  Sobeloff in  United  States v.  Scotland
Brd.  of Educ., 442  F.2d  575  (4th  Cir.)  rev'd,  407  US,  ©
484  -(1972).  Illustrative  of  the  compelling  state  in
terest  doctrine’s  operation  in  school  desegregation  —
cases are the facts in Swann, The  additional  annual
transportation  operating  cost  of  busing  for  deseg-

$100,000,000,  and  the  longest  rides  were  35  minutes  ;

if it is the  one  which  most  effectively  desegregates
the school system. See Brewer v.  School Board, 397
F.2d 37 (1968) ; 456 F.2d 943, 946, n. 6 (4th Cir. 1972)
for cost and mileage  figures in other cases.

a “compelling”  interest.  See  discussion,  supra. .

5,  Effectiveness.

Perhaps the single most important  measure of the
adequacy of a  desegregation  plan is its effectiveness
“The  mandate  of  Brown  II  was  to  desegregate
schools, and we  have said that ‘[t]he measure  of  any ~
desegregation  plan  is  its  effectiveness’”.  Wright  v.
Council  of City  of  Emporia,  407  U.S.  1  (1972).
See also the companion ease to Wright,  United States
».  Scotland  Neck. City Brd, of  Educ,  407. U.S..484
(1972), The test of effectiveness  can best be described
in the terms this Court has used to explain it:,

In  Green,  supra,  this  Court  used  the  term
»  feasible’  and by implication  ‘workable’,  ‘effective
and ‘realistic’  in the mandate  to develop ‘a plan

Swann, supra,  402 U.S. 21.

Clearly,  then,  when  a district  court  reviews  alterna.
tives  presented  to  it,  a  prime  consideration  is  th
relative  effectiveness  of  the  alternative  plans  ty
achieve,
in operation,  the goal  of a  desegregated uni-
tary  system.  But  the  measure  of  effectiveness  is not
limited  to  ‘‘paper’”  effectiveness.  The  plan  must  not
only appear  effective; it must  work  in fact.  Therefore,
district  courts  are charged  with  the  duty  to retain
jurisdiction  of any  desegregation  case  for  a  sufficient
period  of  time  to assure  that  the  plan  selected, in
operation  and  in  fact,  achieves  a  stable,  desegregated
unitary  school  system.  Raney  v.  Board  of Education,
391  U.S.  443,  449  (1968).

One  real.  danger  in  adoption  of  any  plan  is  that
the plan might to so  structured  as  to leave  in existence
substantial  pockets  of  racial  isolation.  When  this 0
curs,  a natural  and  probable  consequence  of that  plan  §
will  be  resegregation;  i.¢.,  continuation  of  a  dual
system  in  different  form.  People  in  the  past  have
moved  in  order  to  isolate  their  school  children  ra-
cially,  and they will continue  to do  so,  as long as any
desegregation  plan  gives  them a real  option  to  move
from-a  pupil assignment
area for an integrated  school
to an assignment  area for  a  segregated  school  within
the  same  cohesive  metropolitan area.  “‘People  gravi-
tate  toward  school  facilities  just  as schools  are lo
cated  in  response  to  the  needs  of  people.”  ee
&
supra,  402  U.S.  at 21.
Hence, the emphasis in’ Swann is on the elimi
tion  of  all  or  nearly  all  disproportionately  one  race
schools.  When  a plan  which  looks  effective  on  {  ape

Tr

fails-beeause  it is  doomed  to  failure  from  the  start,
further  litigation  and  relief  become  necessary.  Dis-
eretionary  choice  of a  plan  which  bears  the  seeds  of
its own  failure  because  it is cheaper  or  more  minimal
is a poor choice:

_.  .  [a]  desegregation  plan  cannot  be  regarded
as  a  proper  exercise  of  a  district  court's  discre-
tion where it is not reasonable,  feasible  and. work-
able.  .  .\,

Wright,  supra, 407  U.S.  at 468-69.
The  Wright,  supra,  and  Scotland  Neck,  supra,
cases  pose  analogous  problems  to  those  present  ‘in
this case.  In  each,  this  Court  reversed  the  Fourth
Circuit  which  had  permitted  both  school  districts  to
adopt  plans  calling  for  creation  of  two  school  sys-
tems  where  there  had  previously  been  but  one.  The
analogy  to intradistrict  establishment  of exclusive  at-
tendance  zones  is  both  obvious  and  complete.  In
Wright,  the  plan  envisaged  creation  of two  districts,
one  of  which  was  52%  black  and  the  other  72%
black,  The alternative  (and. original  plan)  would  have
resulted  in a  single  district  which  was 66% black.
The  Court  refused  to  countenance  this  alternative
plan because  of its probable  and  natural:  effect.  Erec-
tion of new  boundaries  where  no  such lines  previously
existed, occurring  in a  district  where a  dual school
system had long. flourished, “‘must  be judged.  accord-
ing to whether it hinders  or furthers  the  process  of
school desegregation.  Wright,  supra,  407 U.S, at, 460.
The Court summarized the effect it feared by quoting
rom Judge Winter’s dissenting opinion in the Fourth

32

.

.
«  [if  the  establishment  of  an  Emporia  school
district  is not  enjoined,  the  black  students  in the
country  will  watch  as  nearly  one-half  the  total
number  of white  students  in the  country  abandon
the  county  schools  for  a  substantially  whiter  sys.

Wright,  supra,  407  U.S.  at 466.

In a  similar  case,  an adopted  plan  was  remanded

Harrington  v.  Colquitt  Co.  Brd.  of Educ.,  450  F.d
1113  (5th  Cir,  1971).  See  also  Swann  v,  Charlotte.
Mecklenberg  Brd.  of Educ.,  334  Supp.  623  (W.D.N..
1971),  where  Judge  McMillan  addressing  this  prob
lem,  imposed  a  requirement  that  the  adopted  plan
prevent  resegregation:

The  principal  question  now  raised  about.  the
feeder  plan  is  not  the  present  pupil  distribution
per  se  ..  .  Rather,  the  principal  question  is
whether  the schools  will  or  can  remain  desegre
gated—whether  the  plan is likely to be reasonably
stable  in practice  .  .
.  if the plan shows  no  promise
of keeping the schools  reasonably  stable,  the adop-
tion  of  the  plan  may...
statistics  and  map  making.

.  be  only  an  exercise

:

Id, at 627.

To  avoid  the  futility  of engaging  in a  mere  a
cise  in statistics  and  map  making,”  and  to  avoid  cot
stant relitigation  over the same school district, choie
between alternatives  must  obviously take  into
potential  instability  due  to  the  existence 0  or

of racial isolation.  In short, both law  and  common
sense require that effect, in operation, be a  major
factor  in making  a  choice.
These  principles  govern  the  instant  case.  The: Dis-
trict  Court  findings,  which  were  affirmed  by  the
Circuit  Court,  show  that limiting relief to Detroit  city
schools would  be futile.  It would  not result  in unitary
schools,  rather  within'a  very  short  time  it would  re-
ori-
sult in the very same pattern  that now  e  i
d  attend
ties  in  the  metropolitan  Detroit  area  w
virtually  all minority  schools  and  Anglos  in the  same
metropolitan  area  would  attend  virtually  all  Anglo
schools.  Given those findings,  to suggest that considera-
tion  of a metropolitan  remedy  is an  abuse  of discre-
tion evidences  real misunderstanding  of the principles

of  school  desegregation  cases.

CONCLUSION

The  order  of  the  trial  judge  demonstrates  that
feasible  and  effective  desegregation  may  yet  be  im-
plemented  in the  Detroit  metropolitan  area.  The  area
snvolved  is not so  obviously  great nor  is the additional
transportation  so  obviously  significant  as  to  hamper
the  education  process  if this  Court  affirms  the  orders
below,  Indeed,  it is  noteworthy  that  the  strongest
resistance  to  this  order  comes  from  the  suburban
districts  (the  City  of Detroit  is a  respondent)  where
busing is commonplace  because  of the lower population
density,  But  State-created  segregation  should  not  be
tolerated,  especially  where  its  justification  lies  in

34

arbitrary  lines whose  specific  location  serves  only to
mark off areas  aceording  to race.  Nor  should  the map.
date  of  the  Constitution  be  circumvented  because  of
any, public  outerny  over court  orders,  See  Cooper »,

Local school districts  would  indeed  be remiss'if  they  ignored  the
need  for ‘additional  school  facilities  to meet  the educational  needs
of their respective communities.  Moreover, the fact  that construc-
tion of local school district facilities  was paid for  from taxes levied
against ‘only the property  owners  in the  school  district  provides
further rational  basis for constructing  school facilities to  meet the
seed ofthe local school district  community.

SSASTA) of  fas ‘optnion sind Summ 12, 1973.  It is uncontro-
verted that Detroit’s  three percent  (3%) bonding  authority,  with-
out a vote of the people,  had never  been exhausted
by Detroit
when it  was increased  to five percent  (5%) of  the state-equalized
valuation  of taxable  property  within  the district,  thus bringing  it
in percentage  harmony  with  all other  classes  of school  districts
(R. 3547-3549;  17aa-18aa,  25aa-26aa,  31aa,  33aa-34a2).  Having
the greatest aggregate tax base of any school district in the State,
at three percent  (3%)  Detroit  could  issue  bonds  for school  con-
struction  in an amount  exceeding  150 million  dollars,  thus putting
it far ahead of any other school  district  in terms  of actual  dollar
volume  for bonding  without  a  vote of the people.  The increase
from. three percent  (3%) to five percent  (5%)  for Detroit added
another  114 million  dollars  in non-voted  bonding  authority  (R.

9

school  districts  that limits reimbursement  to  students  living ou
side city or village limits.  This statutory  classification  is of long
standing  and totally  unrelated  to  racial motivation.  For  example,
in $34 (b) of  1947  PA 331, the legislature  provided  that  “[a} dd}.
abak aietinints Fo kuihaseianitn  publ aaalt tacsense  to
school districts transporting  pupils within the district but liviig
outside  the village or city corporate  limits. .  .” In 1950,  the black
population  of the City of  Detroit  was only 16.2% of the tota
population  (Pet. A. 21a). Further,  in §77  of 1973  PA  101, MCL.
388.1177, the legislature  has made an initial categorical appropris
tion of $1,500,000.00  for transportation  within cities and villages.
Plaintiffs’  reference  to “ ‘walk-in’  desegregation”  (Bradley
Brief, page 36, n. 34) is put to rest by the District Court’s ruling”
that “...  310,000  of the 780,000 children  within the desegrege
tion area will require transportation  in  order to. accomplish
maximum  actual  desegregation”  (Pet. A.  72a) within  a“.  .
.  rough,
TALUS  40-minute  travel  time  line”  one  way  (Pet. A. 69a), This.
conjectural  one way travel  time projection  is based upon average
driving  time from  a central  point in downtown  Detroit  to  certain  |
lines along a minimum  time  path  (R. Metro Plans,
perimeter
1105-1109).  However,  students
do not live at a central  point within
the school  district 2nd must either  walk to a  central point or be
picked  up by the bus prior to arriving  at a central point for depar-
ture to a school within another  school district.  Moreover,  students  -
would  be transported  during  peak traffic  periods.  Thus, from the |
student’s  point of view, the conjectural  forty (40) minute travel time
one way does not accurately  reflect  the substantially  longer
of his time  that  would  actually  be consumed  daily  by virtue
multi-district  remedy (Deposition  of Harold Wagner, pages 81-83),

dren  are presently  transported  to school  districts  other than thos
in which they reside is without support. Less than 1/2 of 1% of al

district  and receive their education  in another  school district

Board of Education, 402 US. 1,

SECTION  12  OF ACT 48, PA  1970,  HAD NO
MATERIAL  EFFECT  ON THE  RACIAL  COMPOSITION
OF  DETROIT  SCHOOLS  AND NO EFFECT  WHATSOEVER
ON  THE  RACIAL  COMPOSITION  OF  DETROIT
OTHER  SCHOOL  DISTRICTS
VIS-A-VIS

:

“As  with  any  equity  case,  the  nature  of the  violation  deter.
mines  the scope  of the  remedy.”  Swann  v.  Charlotte-Mecklenburg
of Education,  402 U.S.  1, 16 (1971).  When  a case  is pleaded
Board
and tried on the theory  that the Detroit  school  district  is de jure
segregated  with  respect  only  to  the  operation  of  its  schools,
the  District  Court’s  limited  finding  that the  Detroit  school district
has been operated  as a  de  jure  segregated  school  district  does not
constitutionally  permit  a  remedy  extending  beyond  the  Dente
school  district  (Pet. A.  17a, 38a).

It  is within  this  overall  framework  that  this  Court  should
examine  Plaintiffs’  effort.  to  advance  a  theory  of  multi-district
remedy  based  primarily  upon  a philosopher’s  stone  of the  uncon
stitutionality  of  1970  PA  48,  812  (54aa-S8aa),  that  is, a trans
mutation  of something  that  involved  only  the  Detroit  school  dis
trict into something  that involves  not only  the Detroit school
district  but all of the other school  districts  in Michigan  (Bradley
Brief,  pages 52-53, n. 45).  As previously
noted in the earlier brief
of defendants
Milliken, et al. (page 40), 1970  PA  48 consisted of
fourteen  (14)  sections  and was an amendment  to  a prior act, 1969
ee  ee
een  ae
(Detroit  school  district)  to  provide  even  greater  voice  in
school district affairs by persons residingtherein.[1]

with,
Such  broad  contention  is patently  wenn  as begin

of Education  ultimate

16

statute is expressly limited in application  to “emergency”  reorgan-
of primary  and  fourth  class  school  districts:  in  Wayne
ization
County..See M.C.L..  $388.711,  (65aa);  and  Airport Community
‘Schools  v.  State  Board  .of Education,  17.  Mich.  App. 574;  .170
N.W.2d  193 (1969).  Further,  emergency  reorganization  must be
initiated  by either  the local school board or electors of the local
school district. M.C.L.  $388.712°(65aa).  Also,  the  holding  of a
public hearing and a finding  by a state  committee that conditions

instance  delegated  “ultimate control  over alteration  of local school
district boundaries”  to any of the defendants  herein.

Under  Michigan  law,  plenary  authority  over  the  establish-
ment  and  alteration  of local  school  district  boundaries  rests  in the
State  legislature.  Mich  Const  1963,  art  8,  $2  (3aa);  School  Dis-
trict of the City of Lansing  v. State  Board of Education,  367 Mich.
591, 595-596;  116 N.W.2d  866, 868-869  (1962);  Penn School Dis-
trict  No.  7-v.  Lewis  Cass  Intermediate  School  District  Board  of
Education,  14 Mich.  App.  109,  120;  165  N.W.2d  464, 470 (1968).
No party to this case has ever disputed this state of the law.

With respect  to the  area  of school  finance,  neither  the  State
Board  of Education  nor  any  State  officials have  authority  to with-
hold  state  aid  funds  from  local  school  districts  except  for failure
to comply  with  legislative  mandates.  The  power  of appropriation
of state school aid funds to school districts is reposed solely in the
Michigan  legislature  with “*. .  [p]ayments  from  the fund ; .  .  to be
made in full on  a basis scheduled  by legislative  enactment. .  .”
Mich Const 1963, art 9, $11 (Saa), accompanying  Address to the
People, Constitutional  Convention,  Official Record,  1961, Vol. Il,
P 3400.  Further, by statute state school  aid funds “shall be allo-
cated” to school  districts  “as  provided  in  this  act.”  M.C.L.
$388.1111.

Argument is not evidence.  The  Court should note that Plain-
iffs’  “‘confinement”  theory  is not  accompanied  by  any  eviden-
tiary citations  to  the  record  inthis  case.  That such argument is
without  support  is further  attested  to  by the  plain  fact  that  the
District  Court  took  no  proofs with respect  to the establishment  of
the  boundaries  of  any  school  district  or,  with  the  exclusion  of
Detroit,  whether  any school  district  has committed  any  acts of de
jure segregation  (Pet. A. 59a-60a).

Under  Michigan  law  the only requirement  for admission  to a
school within any given, local school district is residence within the
school  district.  This  requirement  applies  equally  to  blacks  and
whites  (M.C.L.. 340.355,  340.356).  There  is no  evidence  in  this
case that any black children  were denied admission  to any school,
or  assigned  to  any  school,  because  of race  or  color,  outside  the

any  such contention.  Swann,  supra,  at
- Moreover, the “student  community”  with respect  to
school district  is the  City  of Detroit.  No one in this
‘desegregation  plan  which  would  mirror  the
Detroit pupil racial  composition  can  be  effected  in  the  Detroit
-.. Plaintiffs’  contention  that  the  rationale  requiring  over-repre-
sented’ electoral  districts  to  participate  in  a  reapportionment
remedy furnishes a constitutional  peg on which  to hang the multi-
school district remedy  in this case is a non-sequitur.!9}  The funda-
mental difference  between  school  districts  and electoral  districts  is
obvious.  The  constitutional  standard  against  which  the validity  of
electoral  districts  is to be measured  is “practicable”  equal popula-
tion. Consequently,  if one  electoral  district  is over  populated,  one
or more other  electoral  districts  must  be  under  populated.  The
constitutional  violation  is thus  interdistrict  in nature,  as  must  be
the remedy.  Here  the constitutional  violations  found  are  limited  to
the operation  of  the  Detroit  school  district,  as  must  be  the
remedy.
Stripped  of rhetorical  facade,  Respondents’  arguments  come
down to  the simple  proposition  that racial imbalance  between  the
Detroit  school  district  and  other  school  districts  gives  rise  to  a
constitutional  violation  and  commands  a  judicially-ordered  dis-
persement  of school children throughout  such geographical  area as
can be traversed  by motor  vehicle  in forty  (40) minutes  to the end
that the schools,  if not  the local communities,  will be racially

area.  Plaintiffs’  eleventh  hour  confinement  theory  is without  gi

INTRODUCTORY  STATEMENT

The  issue  in  this  case  is whether  the  remedy  for
illegal  racial  segregation  of the Detroit  public  schools
may  properly  include a cross-district  pupil assignment
plan  between  the  Detroit  school  district ‘and neighbor-
ing districts,  where  the record  does  not  show  whether

21)

The: United States, intervened.  in the court of appeals
suant to 28 U.S.C. 2403,  because  the  constitutionality
of an
of  Congress  (Section  803  of  P-L 92-318,  86 Stat. 235, 372) had
been called  into question,  The court of. appeals  found it m-
necessary  to consider  the applicability  or  constitutionality  of the

ates  eet quilt  (PE  p  1808), a
>
) pired on  January  1, 1974...

3).

of schools outside  the Detroit district: and where the
suburban  districts have had no  effective  opportunity.
to be heard: on the propriety  of  a metropolitan-wide

“This case began in/Angust  1970 when certain  of the:
primarily  black  parents  and  their  chil-
dren who attended  schools  in the Detroit  public school
system,  sued  city  and state officials,  alleging  that  the,
officials chad: pursued  a  policy  and  practice  of  racial
diserimination  in the  operation  of  the  Detroit  public
schools,  Which  had  resulted  in  a  racially  segregated
school  system.’  The  plaintiffs  sought,  enter  alia, an  or-
der requiring  the defendants  to present a plan “for  the
elimination  of the-racial  identity  of every  school  in the
[Detroit]  system  and  to  maintain  now  and  hereafter
a unitary,  nonracial  school  system’’  (Pet.  App.  15a).
The ease was twice before  the court of  appeals  on  pre-
liminary  matters  (433 F,  2d 897, 438 F.. 2d 945), and a.
trial  on  the  merits  was  held  from  April  to  July  of

1971.

;

In  September  1971,  the  district  court  entered  its
findings  of fact  and conclusions  of law  on  the  issue  of
racial  segregation  in  the  Detroit  schools  (Pet.  App.
17a-39a).  It  found  that  the  Detroit  school  board  had
engaged  in  official  acts  of  racial  discrimination,  that
Act o
*The complaint. also alleged  that a  recently-adopted
the state legislature  unconstitutionally  interfered  with a volun-
Board of
tary plan of desegregation  adopted  by. the Detroit
Education  (Pet. App.  8a-10a).,  The Act was held  unconstitu-
tional by the court of appeals in. an earlier phase of this liti-
gation (433 F, 24 897).

4

had ‘contributed  ‘to racial  segregation  in  the  school
system.  The  board’s  use of  optional  attendance  zones in
areas undergoing  racial  transition and between  schools
of  opposite  predominant  racial  composition  “allowed
whites  to  escape  integration”  (td.  at  25a);  the  board:
transported:  children  on  a  ‘racially  discriminatory
basis  (tbid.);  it gerrymandered  attendance  zones  and
altered  grade  structures  “in  a  manner  which  has had
the  natural,  probable  and  actual  effect  of  continuing
black  and  white  pupils  in racially  segregated  schools”
(id.  at  25a-26a)};  and  it  pursued  discriminatory
school  construction  policies  (td.  at 27a,  28a).

The  court  also  found  that  official  acts  of state  agen-
cies  contributed  to  the racial  segregation  in Detroit’s
schools.  The  enactment  of  state  legislation  rescinding
a  voluntary  plan  of  desegregation  that  had  been
adopted by the  Detroit  board  was  designed,  the  court
held,  “to  impede,  delay  and  minimize  racial  integra-
tion  in  Detroit  schools’”’  (id.  at  28a) ;* and  state  offi-
cials,  as  well  as  the  Detroit  board,  participated  in
racially  discriminatory  decisions  concerning  school
construction  (ibid.).*  The  court-also  concluded  that
Michigan  law  vests  in  the  State  Board  of  Education
“supervision  over  all  public  education’?  (id.  at  36a).

*The  details  of  the  statute  are  set  forth  in  the  opinion  of
the  court  of  appeals  holding  it  unconstitutional  (438 F. m
897).

‘The  court  also  noted  that  state  law  did  not  pievide il

or  authority  for  the  transportation  of pupils in  Detroit,
it  did  provide  for  transportation  of  some  pupils

suburban  districts.  The  court  stated  that  this  “and
nancial  limitations,  such as those on bonding  and the
working
of  the  state  aid  formula  whereby  suburban  districts  were  able
to  make  far  larger  per  pupil  expenditures  despite  less  tax

regation”’  (id. at 43a).°

City  of  Detroit,  but the  larger  Detroit metropolitan

ants’  argument  that  no’  state  action  caused  the  seg-

and  conclusions  on  the  three  “Detroit-only’’  plans

“would  make  the  Detroit  system  more  identifiably  —

from the city. and the system” (id. at 55a). From this

fimits  of the  city”  (id.  at  56a).  It  accordingly  held
that  it “must  look  beyond  the  limits  of  the  Detroit
school  district.for  a  solution  to  the  problem  of  seg-
regation”  (id.  at  57a).  Relying  on  Bradley  v.  School
Board  of the City of Richmond,  Virginia,  338 F. Supp.
67 (E.D, Va.), reversed, 462 F’. 2d 1058  (C.A. 4), affirmed

cal convenience  and  may  not  be used  to deny  constitu-
tional rights” (Pet. App. 57a).

(3)  On June  14, 1972, the district  court  issued  a rul-
ing on  the  desegregation  area  (td.  at  97a-105a)  and
related  findings  and conclusions  (td.  at 59a-96a).  The
court  acknowledged  at the  outset  that  it had “taken  no
proofs  with respect  to the  establishment  of the bound-
aries  of  the  86  public  school  districts  in  the  counties
{in  the  Detroit:area],  nor  on  the  issue  of  whether,
with the exclusion  of the city of Detroit  school  district,
such  school  districts  have  committed  acts  of  de  jure
segregation”  (id.  at  60a).  Nevertheless,  it. designated
53 of the suburban  school  districts  plus Detroit  as the
“desegregation area”  (id.  at  101a)  and  appointed  a
panel  to. prepare  and  submit  ‘‘an  effective  desegrega-
tion  plan” for  the Detroit  schools  that  would  encom-
pass  the  entire  desegregation area  (id.  at 99a).  The
plan. was to be based. on 15 clusters, each containing
part  of the  Detroit  system  and  two  or  more  suburban
districts  (V  App.  111-115),  and  was  to  “achieve  the

the District  Court”  (td. at 173a).°

is thus  “within  the  equity  powers of

The court of appeals  expressed  no views  on the pro
priety  of  the  district  court’s  “desegregation  area.”  It
held  that  all  suburban  school  districts  that  might  he
affected  by  any  metropolitan-wide  remedy  should  he
made  parties  to  the  case  on  remand  and be: given an
opportunity  to be heard  with respect  to the scope and
implementation  of such a remedy  (id. at 177a).  Under
the  terms  of  the  remand,  however,  the  district  court
need
not receive  further  evidence  on the issue of segre-
gation  in  the  Detroit  schools  or  on  the  propriety  ofa
Detroit-only  remedy  (td.  at  178a).

It is our  view  that  the  remedy  for  unconstitutional
ségregation  of  the  public  schools  in  a  school  district
can  properly  extend  beyond  the  boundaries of the dis-
trict  only  where  the  violation  has  directly  altered  or
substantially  affected  the  racial  composition  of schools
outside  the  district  and  only  to the  extent  necessary  to
eliminate  the  segregative  effects  of  the  violation.
Where  the  schools  of  only  one  district  have  been  af-
feeted,  there  is no  constitutional  requirement  that the
relief  include  a  balancing  of the  racial  composition of
that  district’s  schools  with  those  of  surrounding
;
districts.
The  record  does  not  support  the  ruling of the court

unlike  Michigan's  gave the local  boards exclusive  power  to operate
